entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-2]
entry.content:
Adding CDS Annotations to Build the Fiori UI

In Part 1 , we built and tested our CAP application locally using SQLite. The OData services were working, but when we clicked "Fiori preview" the UI showed raw data with no field labels, no filters, and no meaningful layout.

In this Part, we will add CDS Annotations to shape the Fiori Elements UI —  defining what fields appear, how they are labelled, how the list and detail pages are structured, and which fields get dropdown value helps.

The best part: we do all of this entirely in CDS — no UI5 code needed. Fiori Elements reads these annotations and automatically renders the UI.


WHAT ARE CDS ANNOTATIONS?

CDS Annotations are metadata decorators that you add to your entities or service projections. They tell the Fiori Elements framework how to render the UI — which columns appear in the list, what sections exist on the detail page, which fields should show a value help dropdown, and how labels should read in the browser.

We organize our annotations into three separate files, each focused on one concern:

labels.cds → Human-readable field labels for UI display
layout.cds → Page layout: list columns, detail sections, filters
valuehelps.cds → Dropdown/value help configuration for input fields

Keeping them separate makes the code easier to read and maintain as the application grows.

 

STEP 1 — CREATE THE ANNOTATIONS FOLDER AND FILES

Inside the "app" folder of your CAP project, create a new folder named: annotations

Your folder structure will look like this:

incident-management/
├── app/
│ └── annotations/
│ ├── labels.cds
│ ├── layout.cds
│ └── valuehelps.cds
├── db/
└── srv/

Now create each of the three files with the content below.


a) app/annotations/labels.cds

This file assigns human-readable labels to every field in the Incidents, Customers, Addresses, Status, and Urgency entities. Without these, Fiori Elements falls back to the raw technical field names (e.g., "status_code" instead of "Status").

using {sap.capire.incidents as my} from '../../db/schema';

annotate my.Incidents with @title: 'Incidents' {
    ID           @title          : 'ID';
    createdAt    @title          : 'Created At';
    createdBy    @title          : 'Created By';
    modifiedAt   @title          : 'Modified At';
    modifiedBy   @title          : 'Modified By';
    customer     @title: 'Customer'  : {
        Text           : customer.name,
        TextArrangement: #TextOnly
    };
    title        @title          : 'Title';
    urgency      @title: 'Urgency'   : {
        Text           : urgency.descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
    status       @title: 'Status'    : {
        Text           : status.descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
    conversation @title          : 'Conversation';
}

annotate my.Customers {
    ID           @title: 'Customer ID';
    createdAt    @title: 'Created At';
    createdBy    @title: 'Created By';
    modifiedAt   @title: 'Modified At';
    modifiedBy   @title: 'Modified By';
    firstName    @title: 'First Name';
    lastName     @title: 'Last Name';
    name         @title: 'Name';
    email        @title: 'Email';
    phone        @title: 'Phone';
    creditCardNo @title: 'Credit Card No';
    incidents    @title: 'Incidents';
    addresses    @title: 'Addresses';
}

annotate my.Addresses with @title: 'Addresses' {
    customer      @title         : 'Customer';
    city          @title         : 'City';
    postCode      @title         : 'Post Code';
    streetAddress @title         : 'Street Address';
}

annotate my.Status {
    code        @title: 'Status Code'  : {
        Text           : descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
    criticality @title: 'Criticality';
    descr       @title: 'Status Description';
}

annotate my.Urgency {
    code  @title: 'Urgency Code'  : {
        Text           : descr,
        TextArrangement: #TextFirst
    };
}

b) app/annotations/layout.cds
This file defines the full UI layout for the ProcessorService.Incidents entity. It controls both the List Report page and the Object (detail) page.

using {ProcessorService} from '../../srv/incidents_processor';

annotate ProcessorService.Incidents with : {
    HeaderInfo         : {
        TypeName      : 'Incident',
        TypeNamePlural: 'Incidents',
        Title         : {Value: title},
        Description   : {Value: customer.name}
    },
    Facets             : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.CollectionFacet',
            Label : 'Overview',
            Facets: [
                {
                    Label : 'General Information',
                    $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
                    Target: '@UI.FieldGroup#GenInfo',
                },
                {
                    Label : 'Status',
                    $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
                    Target: '@UI.FieldGroup#Status',
                }
            ],

        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
            Label : 'Conversations',
            Target: 'conversation/@UI.LineItem',
        }
    ],

    FieldGroup #Status : {
        Label: 'Details',
        $Type: 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            {
                Value                    : status_code,
                Label                    : 'Status',
                Criticality              : status.criticality,
                CriticalityRepresentation: #WithIcon,
            },
            {
                Value                    : urgency_code,
                Label                    : 'Urgency',
                Criticality              : urgency.criticality,
                CriticalityRepresentation: #WithoutIcon,
            }
        ]
    },

    FieldGroup #GenInfo: {
        Label: 'Overview',
        $Type: 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            {Value: customer.name},
            {Value: title}
        ]
    },

    LineItem           : [
        {
            Value         : title,
            .Importance: #High
        },
        {
            Value         : customer.name,
            .Importance: #High,
        },
        {
            Value                    : status_code,
            Criticality              : status.criticality,
            CriticalityRepresentation: #WithIcon,
            .Importance           : #High,
        },
        {
            Value                    : urgency_code,
            .Importance           : #Low,
            Criticality              : urgency.criticality,
            CriticalityRepresentation: #WithoutIcon,
        },
    ],

    SelectionFields    : [
        customer_ID,
        status_code,
        urgency_code
    ],
};

annotate ProcessorService.Incidents.conversation with : {LineItem: [
    {
        Value: message,
        Label: 'Message'
    },
    {Value: author},
    {Value: timestamp}
], }


c) app/annotations/valuehelps.cds

This file adds dropdown / value help popups to input fields on the Incidents entity, making data entry easier and consistent.

using {ProcessorService} from '../../srv/incidents_processor';

annotate ProcessorService.Incidents with {
    status   .ValueListWithFixedValues;
    urgency  .ValueListWithFixedValues;
    customer .ValueList: {
        CollectionPath: 'Customers',
        Parameters    : [
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut',
                LocalDataProperty: customer_ID,
                ValueListProperty: 'ID'
            },
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty: 'name',
            },
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty: 'email',
            },
            {
                $Type            : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty: 'phone',
            },
        ]
    }
}


STEP 2 — RUN LOCALLY WITH SQLITE AND PREVIEW THE UI

Now that annotations are in place, re-deploy the schema and restart the server:

cds deploy --to sqlite
cds watch

Open your browser at: http://localhost:4004

You will see the familiar CDS welcome page. This time, click "Fiori preview" next to the "Incidents" entity under the /odata/v4/processor service.

 

The List Report and Object page now renders with full layout and labels.

 

 


SUMMARY

In Part 2, we successfully:

✔ Created an "annotations" folder under app/ with three CDS files
✔ Added field labels for all entities (labels.cds)
✔ Defined the List Report columns, filter bar, Object Page layout and detail page sections (layout.cds)
✔ Configured value help dropdowns for Status, Urgency, and Customer (valuehelps.cds)
✔ Previewed the fully rendered Fiori Elements UI locally with SQLite

In Part 3 , we will set up SAP HANA Cloud on BTP — creating the database instance and mapping it to our Cloud Foundry space.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-3]
entry.content:
Setting Up SAP HANA Cloud on BTP

In Part 2 , we added CDS Annotations and previewed the Fiori UI locally with SQLite. Now it is time to move to the cloud database layer.

In this Part, we will set up SAP HANA Cloud on SAP BTP so our CAP application can use it as the production database. This involves several one-time BTP configuration steps before we can deploy anything.

Here is what we will do in this part:

1. Verify SAP HANA Cloud is entitled at the Global Account level
2. Assign the HANA Cloud service plans to your Subaccount
3. Subscribe to HANA Cloud Tools via the Service Marketplace
4. Assign the required HANA Cloud roles to your user
5. Create a SAP HANA Cloud database instance
6. Map the Cloud Foundry environment to the HANA Cloud instance

All of these steps are done in the SAP BTP Cockpit and SAP HANA Cloud Central.

STEP 1 — VERIFY HANA CLOUD ENTITLEMENTS IN THE GLOBAL ACCOUNT

Before creating any HANA Cloud resources, confirm that your Global Account has the SAP HANA Cloud service entitlement available.

In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to:

Global Account → Entitlements → Service Assignments

In the search box, type "HANA" to filter the services.

 You should see the services available.

 

If you see these listed, your Global Account is entitled.

Proceed to Step 2.

NOTE: If SAP HANA Cloud does not appear here, contact your BTP Global Account administrator to enable the entitlement.


STEP 2 — ASSIGN HANA CLOUD SERVICE PLANS TO YOUR SUBACCOUNT

Entitlements available at the Global Account level must be explicitly assigned to a Subaccount before they can be used there.

Navigate to:

Global Account → Entitlements → Entity Assignments

Filter by your subaccount (e.g., "trial") and locate the SAP HANA Cloud service. Click "Edit" and add the required service plans to your subaccount.

- Search for "hana" in the services list on the left
- Select "SAP HANA Cloud" from the results
- On the right panel, check the following plans and Click "Add Service Plans" to confirm

 

After saving, the Entity Assignments page will show SAP HANA Cloud with the assigned plans for your subaccount.

 

STEP 3 — SUBSCRIBE TO HANA CLOUD TOOLS IN THE SERVICE MARKETPLACE

SAP HANA Cloud is managed through a browser-based graphical tool called SAP HANA Cloud Central. To access it, you must first subscribe to the "hana-cloud-tools" application in your subaccount's Service Marketplace.

Navigate to your Subaccount, then go to: Services → Service Marketplace

Search for "HANA CLOUD". Click on the "SAP HANA Cloud" tile.

 

 

In the right panel, click "Create". A wizard dialog will open.

 

 Click "Create". This creates a subscription to HANA Cloud Central.

 

Navigate to:  Services → Instances and Subscriptions

You should now see "SAP HANA Cloud" listed under Subscriptions with Status: "Subscribed".

 

STEP 4 — ASSIGN HANA CLOUD ROLES TO YOUR USER

Before you can open HANA Cloud Central, your BTP user needs the correct role collections assigned. Without them, you will see a "Not authorized" error when trying to access HANA Cloud Central.

 

 

 

The required SAP HANA Cloud role collections are:
https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud/sap-hana-cloud-administration-guide/role-collections-for-sap-hana-cloud

 

To assign these roles, navigate to your Subaccount:

 

Security → Users

Click on your user  to open the details panel.
Click "Assign Role Collection".

 

 

In the "Assign Role Collection" dialog, search for "SAP HANA CLOUD".
Select all the required role collections:

 

Click "Assign Role Collection" to save.

 

STEP 5 — CREATE THE SAP HANA CLOUD DATABASE INSTANCE

Now open SAP HANA Cloud Central. You can access it from:

Services → Instances and Subscriptions → SAP HANA Cloud → "Go to Application"

HANA Cloud Central will open in a new browser tab. The Instances page will show "No instances" — we need to create one.

 

Click "Create Instance". A multi-step wizard opens with 8 steps.

1

 

2

 

3

 

4

 

5

 

6

 

7

 

8

 

After creation, HANA Cloud Central will show both instances as "Running":

 

STEP 6 — MAP THE CLOUD FOUNDRY ENVIRONMENT TO THE HANA CLOUD INSTANCE

The HANA Cloud instance was created but it needs to be mapped to your Cloud Foundry organization and space. This mapping is what enables CAP to create HDI (HANA Deployment Infrastructure) containers for schema deployment when you run cds deploy --to hana.

Click on the "SHC_DB" instance to open its details. 

 

Click "Manage Configuration" → go to the "Instance Mapping" tab.

Click "Add Mapping". You will be redirected to sign into the CF environment.

 

Sign in with your default identity provider credentials. After authentication, return to the Instance Mapping tab and click "Add Mapping" again.

 

Now a new row appears. Fill in the mapping:

Click "Review and Save" to confirm the mapping. The HANA Cloud instance is now connected to your Cloud Foundry space and ready to receive HDI container deployments from the CAP application.

 


SUMMARY

In Part 3, we successfully:

✔ Verified SAP HANA Cloud entitlements at the Global Account level
✔ Assigned HANA Cloud service plans to the trial subaccount
✔ Subscribed to SAP HANA Cloud Tools (hana-cloud-tools) via Service Marketplace
✔ Assigned all required SAP HANA Cloud role collections to our user
✔ Created a SAP HANA Cloud database instance (SHC_DB) with a linked Data Lake (SHC_DL) using the free tier
✔ Mapped the Cloud Foundry organization and space to the HANA instance to enable HDI container creation

The SAP HANA Cloud infrastructure is now fully set up and ready. In Part 4, we will connect the CAP application to this HANA Cloud instance and deploy the backend to Cloud Foundry.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-4]
entry.content:
Connecting CAP application to HANA Cloud and Deploying  to Cloud Foundry

In Part 3 , we created the SAP HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) on BTP and mapped it to our Cloud Foundry space.

In this Part, we will:

- Update package.json with the correct CDS profiles for each environment
- Run cds add hana to wire the CAP project for HANA Cloud deployment
- Build the MTA archive using mbt build
- Deploy the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
- Verify the deployment and explore the HANA tables in DB Explorer
- Run the app locally in hybrid mode — connected to the deployed HANA instance

PREREQUISITES

Make sure the following tools are installed before proceeding:

- Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) — logged in to your BTP CF environment: cf login
- MBT (Multi-Target Application Build Tool): npm install -g mbt
- @sap/cds-dk (already installed from Part 1)


STEP 1 — UPDATE PACKAGE.JSON WITH CDS PROFILES

The package.json in the CAP project already has a "cds" section that defines how the app connects to the database in each environment. We need to make sure the three profiles are correctly configured:

development → uses SQLite (local, in-file database — for cds watch)
hybrid → uses SAP HANA Cloud (local app, cloud database — for testing)
production → uses SAP HANA Cloud (fully deployed on CF)

Update or verify the "cds" section in your package.json:

{
  "name": "incident-management",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "Incident Management CAP Application",
  "repository": "<Add your repository here>",
  "license": "UNLICENSED",
  "private": true,
  "dependencies": {
    "@cap-js/hana": "^2",
    "@sap/cds": "^9",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "@sap/xssec": "^4",
    "express": "^5"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@cap-js/sqlite": "^2",
    "@sap/cds-dk": "^9",
    "cds-plugin-ui5": "^0.17.0"
  },
  "cds": {
    "requires": {
      "[development]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "sqlite"
        }
      },
      "[hybrid]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      },
      "[production]": {
        "auth": {
          "kind": "dummy"
        },
        "db": {
          "kind": "hana-cloud"
        }
      }
    }
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "cds-serve",
    "watch": "cds watch",
    "watch-incidents": "cds watch --open incidents/index.html?sap-ui-xx-viewCache=false --livereload false"
  }
}

WHY THREE PROFILES?
This pattern lets you use the same codebase across all environments without changing any code — just switch the profile.

SQLite keeps local development fast and offline.

Hybrid lets you test against real HANA data without a full CF deployment.

Production is used when the app runs on Cloud Foundry.

 

STEP 2 — RUN cds add hana

Run the following command in the terminal from inside the project folder:

cds add hana

This single command does several important things to prepare your CAP project for SAP HANA Cloud deployment:

- Adds the @cap-js/hana dependency to package.json (HANA database adapter)
- Generates or updates the mta.yaml file with the correct MTA module definitions for CF deployment
- Adds an HDI container resource definition to mta.yaml
- Configures the production CDS profile to use hana-cloud as the db kind

After running this command, your mta.yaml will define three components:

1. incident-management-srv (Node.js module — the CAP OData service)
- Deployed as a CF application
- Provides: srv-api (the service URL consumed by the Fiori app later)
- Requires: incident-management-db (the HDI container)

2. incident-management-db-deployer (hdb module — the schema deployer)
- A one-time deployer app that pushes the CDS schema to HANA
- Runs during deployment then stops (Stopped state is normal)
- Requires: incident-management-db

3. incident-management-db (resource — the HDI container)
- Type: com.sap.xs.hdi-container
- Service: hana, Plan: hdi-shared
- This is the actual schema container created inside your HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) that was set up in Part 3 


STEP 3 — BUILD THE MTA ARCHIVE

Run the MBT build command to compile the project into a deployable archive:

mbt build

This command:
- Reads the mta.yaml configuration
- Runs `npm install` and `npx cds build --production` to compile the CDS model
- Packages all modules into a single .mtar archive file

The output archive is created at:

mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

 

STEP 4 — DEPLOY TO CLOUD FOUNDRY

Make sure you are logged in to CF and targeting the correct space:

cf login -a <api endpoint>
cf target -o <your-org> -s dev

Now deploy the MTA archive:

cf deploy mta_archives/incident-management_1.0.0.mtar

CF will:
1. Create the HDI container service instance (incident-management-db)
2. Deploy and run the db-deployer to push the schema to HANA
3. Start the CAP service application (incident-management-srv)

Once deployment completes, navigate to your CF space in BTP Cockpit:

Trial Home →   f030ff54trial   → trial   → dev → Applications

You will see two applications in the "dev" space:

 

STEP 5 — VERIFY THE HDI CONTAINER INSTANCE

Navigate to:

Space: dev → Services → Instances

You should see the HDI container service instance that was automatically created during deployment:

 This HDI container lives inside the SHC_DB HANA Cloud instance we created in Part 3. CAP automatically created it during the cf deploy step.

 

STEP 6 — EXPLORE THE HANA TABLES IN DB EXPLORER

Now let's verify the schema was deployed correctly by exploring the tables in SAP HANA Database Explorer.

Open HANA Cloud Central → click "Open" on the SHC_DB instance →
select "SAP HANA Database Explorer".

In Database Explorer, the left panel shows your SHC-DB instance connected as DBADMIN. To view the HDI container tables, click the "+" (Add Instance) icon.

 

Select "HDI Container" as the Instance Type, filter by your org (f030ff54trial) and space (dev).

Select "incident-management-db" and click OK.

 

The HDI container is now added to the left panel. Expand it to see the Tables folder. You will find all the CDS entities have been deployed as HANA tables.

Click on SAP_CAPIRE_INCIDENTS_INCIDENTS to inspect its structure:


STEP 7 — RUNNING IN HYBRID MODE

Now that the CAP app is deployed and the HDI container exists in BTP, you can run your local development server connected directly to the cloud HANA database. This is the "hybrid" mode — your app runs locally but reads and writes real HANA Cloud data.

This is very useful for testing local code changes against production data before deploying again.

Step 1 — Bind your local project to the HDI container service instance:

cds bind -2 incident-management-db:key

What this does:
- Connects to Cloud Foundry and retrieves the service key for the "incident-management-db" HDI container instance
- Creates a service key on BTP (if one doesn't exist)
- Saves the HANA connection credentials (host, schema, user, password) into .cdsrc-private.json under the "hybrid" profile
- The .cdsrc-private.json file is local only — never commit it to git

Step 2 — Start the server in hybrid mode:

cds watch --profile hybrid

The terminal confirms the app is now connected to the real HANA Cloud database, showing:
- The HANA host and schema ID
- Driver: com.sap.db.jdbc.Driver (JDBC connection to HANA)
- Both ProcessorService and AdminService are being served

Your app on localhost:4004 now reads and writes directly to the HANA Cloud instance in BTP — the Fiori preview will show and save real data.


SUMMARY

In Part 4, we successfully:

✔ Configured package.json with three CDS profiles: development (SQLite), hybrid (local + HANA Cloud), production (CF + HANA)
✔ Ran cds add hana to wire the project for HANA Cloud and generate mta.yaml
✔ Built the deployable MTA archive using mbt build
✔ Deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry using cf deploy
✔ Verified the two CF apps: incident-management-srv (Started) and incident-management-db-deployer (Stopped — expected)
✔ Confirmed the HDI container instance was created with 2 bindings
✔ Explored the HANA tables and seed data in SAP HANA Database Explorer
✔ Connected the local app to the HANA Cloud instance using hybrid mode

The CAP backend is now live on Cloud Foundry and backed by SAP HANA Cloud.
In Part 5 , we will build the Fiori Elements UI application and connect it to this deployed CAP service.

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-6]
entry.content:
Building and Deploying the Fiori App to HTML5 Repository

In the previous parts we:

✔ Built and tested the CAP backend locally (Part 1  & Part 2 )
✔ Set up SAP HANA Cloud on BTP (Part 3 )
✔ Deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry (Part 4 )
✔ Configured the BTP Destination to point to the CAP service (Part 5 )

In this Part, we will:
- Generate the Fiori Elements app using the SAP Fiori Application Generator
- Add deployment configuration for Cloud Foundry and the HTML5 Repository
- Build and deploy the Fiori app as a standalone HTML5 application
- Access the fully working end-to-end application via SAP Build Work Zone

 

PREREQUISITES

Before proceeding, make sure the following are in place:

- VS Code with the SAP Fiori Tools Extension Pack installed / Business Application Studio (BAS)
- Cloud Foundry CLI logged in (cf login)
- The CAP backend deployed and running (from Part 4)
- The destination "destination-trial" configured in BTP (from Part 5)

> IMPORTANT — You also need the following active subscriptions in your BTP Subaccount before you can access the deployed Fiori app:
> - SAP HTML5 Application Repository Service
> - SAP Cloud Identity Services
> - SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (for the Launchpad)

> Follow the step-by-step guide here  to enable these.

 

PART A — DEVELOPING THE FIORI UI APP

STEP 1 — OPEN THE SAP FIORI APPLICATION GENERATOR

In VS Code/BAS, press Ctrl+Shift+P and search for: SAP Fiori: Open Application Generator

The SAP Fiori Generator panel opens with a multi-step wizard on the left.

 

STEP 2 — SELECT THE TEMPLATE

On the Template Selection screen, choose: List Report Page

This template generates a two-page Fiori Elements app — a List Report for browsing records and an Object Page for viewing/editing individual records.
It uses the CDS Annotations we defined in Part 2 to render the UI automatically.

 Click "Next >".

 

STEP 3 — DATA SOURCE AND SERVICE SELECTION

Data Source:  Connect to an OData Service

 Click "Next >". The generator fetches the metadata from the live service.

STEP 4 — ENTITY SELECTION

Once the metadata is loaded, select:

Main Entity: Incidents
Navigation Entity: None
Generate Annotations: Yes
Table Type: Responsive

The generator will pick up the CDS Annotations we defined in Part 2 automatically since "Generate Annotations" is set to Yes.

Click "Next >".

STEP 5 — PROJECT ATTRIBUTES

Fill in the project details:

 Click "Finish". The generator scaffolds the complete Fiori Elements project in the specified folder. This creates the "incidentmanagement" app folder with webapp/, manifest.json, xs-app.json, and all config files.

STEP 6 — RUN AND TEST APP LOCALLY

npm run start-noflp

 

PART B — DEPLOYING THE FIORI APP TO HTML5 REPOSITORY

STEP 7 — ADD DEPLOYMENT CONFIGURATION

Navigate into the Fiori app folder and run:

npm run deploy-config

This command launches an interactive prompt to configure the deployment.


Answer the prompts as follows:

Target environment: Cloud Foundry
Destination name: destination-trial
App Router: Managed Application Router

Running this command generates/updates two key files:

mta.yaml → Multi-Target Application descriptor for CF deployment.
Defines three CF resources to be created:
- incidentmanagement-destination-service (destination, lite)→ includes a built-in "ui5" destination for SAPUI5 resources
- incidentmanagement-uaa (XSUAA, application plan)→ OAuth2 security for the app, configured via xs-security.json
- incidentmanagement-repo-host (html5-apps-repo, app-host)→ the HTML5 Application Repository bucket that stores the app

ui5-deploy.yaml → UI5 build configuration.
Packages the app into incidentmanagement.zip (excluding test/ and localService/ folders) and bundles xs-app.json into the archive for AppRouter routing rules.

 

STEP 8 — BUILD THE MTA ARCHIVE

Run the build command:

npm run build:mta

This command:
- Runs npm install and npm run build:cf to compile the UI5 application
- Packages the compiled app into incidentmanagement.zip
- Bundles everything into a deployable MTA archive: mta_archives/incidentmanagement_0.0.1.mtar

 

STEP 9 — DEPLOY TO CLOUD FOUNDRY

Deploy the Fiori app MTA archive to Cloud Foundry:

cf deploy mta_archives/incidentmanagement_0.0.1.mtar

During deployment, CF will automatically:
1. Create the XSUAA service instance (incidentmanagement-xsuaa-service)
2. Create the HTML5 App Repository host (incidentmanagement-html5-service)
3. Create the Destination service instance (incidentmanagement-destination-service)— including the built-in "ui5" destination for SAPUI5 CDN resources
4. Uploads the Fiori app zip to the HTML5 Repository
5. Register the app's destination content (html5 repo host + XSUAA token exchange)

STEP 10 — VERIFY AND ACCESS LIVE APPLICATION IN HTML5 REPO

In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Subaccount and click:

HTML5 Applications (in the left navigation)

You will see the deployed Fiori app listed under the Managed Application Router provided by SAP Build Work Zone:

 

Click on "incidentmanagement" in the HTML5 Applications page to open the app in SAP Build Work Zone Launchpad.

The Fiori Elements List Report opens in the browser — now running fully in the cloud, reading live data from SAP HANA Cloud via the CAP service:

 
SERIES SUMMARY:

Over 6 parts, we built and deployed a complete, production-grade Incident Management application on SAP BTP using modern SAP technologies:

Part 1  → CAP Node.js project with domain model, OData services,CSV seed data — running locally on SQLite

Part 2  → CDS Annotations for the Fiori Elements UI:field labels, list/object page layout, value helps

Part 3  → SAP HANA Cloud instance (SHC_DB) on BTP — entitlements,roles, instance creation, CF environment mapping

Part 4  → CAP backend deployed to Cloud Foundry, HDI containercreated, schema deployed, hybrid mode with cds bind

Part 5  → BTP Destination (destination-trial) configured to bridge the Fiori app to the CAP service URL

Part 6 (This Part) → Fiori Elements app generated, configured for HTML5 Repo, deployed to Cloud Foundry — live end-to-end on BTP

The result is a fully decoupled architecture where the CAP backend and the Fiori frontend are deployed and scaled independently a pattern recommended by SAP for modern BTP applications.

I hope this series helped you understand how CAP, HANA Cloud, Fiori Elements, and BTP services come together to build a real application.

 In the coming parts, we will get to know about configuring the App Router, understanding xs-security.json, adding validations, implementing custom actions, securing our app with XSUAA, and even more. Stay tuned!

entry.title: Fullstack CAP Application with HANA Cloud (Decoupled Architecture) [Part-5]
entry.content:
Configuring the Destination in BTP

In Part 4 , we deployed the CAP backend to Cloud Foundry. It is now live and accessible via a public CF route URL.

In this Part, we will configure a BTP Destination — a named connection entry that acts as a pointer from BTP services to the deployed CAP backend URL.

This destination is the critical link between the Fiori Elements app (which we will deploy to the HTML5 Application Repository in Part 6) and the CAP OData service running on Cloud Foundry.


WHY IS A DESTINATION NEEDED?

In a decoupled architecture, the Fiori app and the CAP backend are deployed independently:

Fiori App (HTML5 Repo) ──► xs-app.json routes ──► Destination ──► CAP Service (CF)

When the browser loads the Fiori app and makes OData calls (e.g., GET /odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents), those requests are routed through the AppRouter via xs-app.json. The routing rule in xs-app.json says:

Any request matching ^/odata/(.*) → forward to "destination-trial"

The destination named "destination-trial" then resolves to the actual HTTPS URL of the CAP service on Cloud Foundry. This way, the Fiori app never needs to hardcode the backend URL — it just uses the destination name.

 

STEP 1 — NAVIGATE TO DESTINATIONS IN BTP COCKPIT

In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Subaccount and go to:

Connectivity → Destinations

The Destinations page will open showing an empty list (All: 0) since no destinations have been created yet.

The left panel shows the context level:
trial - Destinations → trial (Read and Write access)

 

STEP 2 — CREATE A NEW DESTINATION

Click the "Create" button. A small dialog appears with three options:

From Scratch → Create a new destination manually by filling in the form From File → Import a destination from a previously exported config file Service Instance → Auto-create a destination from a BTP service instance

Select "From Scratch" since we will fill in the details manually.

 

STEP 3 — FIND THE CAP SERVICE URL

Before filling in the destination form, you need the URL of the deployed CAP service. Navigate to:

Space: dev → Applications → incident-management-srv → Application Overview

In the "Mapped Routes" section on the right, you will see the public CF route assigned to the CAP service.

  Copy this route URL — you will paste it as the destination URL in the next step.

STEP 4 — FILL IN THE DESTINATION DETAILS

Back on the Destinations page, fill in the form.

 

 

 WHY NoAuthentication?
In this tutorial setup with a trial account, we are using "dummy" auth in the CAP service (as configured in package.json).

For a productive setup, you would use OAuth2ClientCredentials or OAuth2UserTokenExchange with an XSUAA service instance to secure the backend properly.

Click "Save" to create the destination.

The destination is now listed in the Destinations page:

Name: destination-trial | Type: HTTP

HOW THE DESTINATION IS USED IN THE FIORI APP

The Fiori app references this destination by name in two key files:

xs-app.json (AppRouter routing rules):
- Any request to /odata/* is forwarded to "destination-trial"
- This routes all OData calls from the Fiori UI to the CAP service
- Authentication is handled via XSUAA

manifest.json (OData data source):
- The mainService data source points to /odata/v4/incidents-processor/
- This path matches the /odata/* routing rule in xs-app.json, so it will automatically be forwarded to the destination-trial destination and reach the CAP ProcessorService on Cloud Foundry

> In short: Fiori app calls /odata/v4/incidents-processor/Incidents
> → xs-app.json routes it to destination-trial
> → destination-trial resolves to the CF route of incident-management-srv
> → CAP service responds with HANA Cloud data


SUMMARY

In Part 5, we successfully:

✔ Navigated to the Destinations section in the BTP Subaccount
✔ Created a new HTTP destination named "destination-trial" from scratch
✔ Used the CF mapped route of incident-management-srv as the destination URL
✔ Understood how xs-app.json and manifest.json in the Fiori app reference this destination to route OData calls to the CAP backend

The destination is now the live bridge between the Fiori frontend and the CAP backend. In Part 6 , we will deploy the Fiori Elements app to the HTML5 Application Repository and access the complete end-to-end application.

entry.title: From Form to Tile: Add an SAP BPA start form in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
Introduction:

This guide shows how to surface an SAP Build Process Automation (SAP BPA) start form as a tile in SAP Build Work Zone, so users can trigger workflows directly from a Work Zone site. The steps apply in general, whether or not your tenant is integrated with SAP IPD Collaboration

You’ll learn how to collect the required IDs, configure the app parameters and navigation, adjust the tile’s visualization and translations, and grant role access. By the end, your site will offer a reliable, one-click entry point to launch your SAP BPA workflow.

This article provides a detailed, screenshot-rich walkthrough of the official Help documentation: see the Help page.

 

Prerequisites:

To follow this guide, you need a deployed workflow in SAP BPA with a form as the trigger and a Work Zone tenant with SAP IPD Collaboration enabled.

For more information about creating a workflow and setting up SAP Build Work Zone, please refer to the following blogs:

  • Developing Processes in SAP Build Process Automation for Integrated Product Development by @Alexandra_Rong
  • SAP Build Work Zone in Integrated Product Development: A Step-by-Step Setup Guide by @Aylinborzan

 

Step 1: Get the required information

To create a tile on your preferred Work Zone site, you need details from the SAP BPA start form. The required IDs are available as part of a link in the workflow where the form is configured as the trigger. Make sure you are viewing the deployed version of the workflow. The IDs do not change with each release cycle; they only change if you modify the trigger. Therefore, if you adjust the workflow or update fields in the form (without changing the trigger), you do not need to update the IDs in the Work Zone content settings.

In the deployed version of the workflow, click the trigger that uses the form. A details panel opens on the right. There you’ll find two links: one opens the form in a new window so you can start the workflow, and the other provides configuration details. The configuration link is required to connect to SAP Build Work Zone. Copy the link and paste it into a notes app.

 

Step 2: Check if “Process Trigger“ is already visible in “Content Manager“

To check if the “Process Trigger“ app is already visible in “Content Manager“, navigate to the “Content Manager“ in the SAP Build Work Zone.

There are multiple apps with the name “Process Trigger“, so make sure that it is from the channel “HTML5 Apps“. If you can see it in “Content Manager,” continue with Step 3.

Otherwise click on “Content Explorer“ on the upper right side next to the create button. There click on “HTML5 Apps“ with the subtitle “saas_approuter“.

Then select the app “Process Trigger“ and click on “Add“ in the upper right corner.

Afterwards, go back to “Content Manager“ and verify that “Process Trigger” now appears in the list.

 

Step 3: Create the app in SAP Build Work Zone

To create the app in SAP Build Work Zone, go to “Content Manager“ via the left navigation.

There you can see all sites, apps, and roles you have already created. Open the app “Process Trigger“ from the channel “HTML5 Apps“ by clicking the row.

This app is a template for the tile you want to create. You will see information such as your subaccount ID, which will be reused for your tile. Click the “Create a Local Copy” button in the upper-right corner to copy the template.

A new app based on the template opens. Click “Edit” in the upper-right corner to add all required information for your start form.

First, change the “Application Title“ and enter a description. The application title will be used as the tile title later.

Then go to the “Navigation” tab. There you find intent and parameters as section. Change the “Semantic Object“ and “Action“ to a fitting name for your form and enter the default value for the “uri“ parameter. The default value is the full URL you copied from SAP BPA, including all IDs.

Next, adjust the tile visualization in the “Visualization” tab. You can change the subtitle, add additional information, and update the displayed icon. The tile title matches the application title you set earlier.

In the “Translation“ tab, you can add translations for your tile. Make sure to update the translated text for English at minimum, since the translation is copied from the template.

In the “Additional Info” tab, you can see who created the app and when it was last changed. No action is required here.

Now save your changes by clicking on the button ”Save” in the upper right corner. 

Your tile has been successfully created.

 

Step 4: Add the tile to the site

After creating the tile for your start form, add it to your site which you can select in the ”Content Manager”.

Open the site where you want the tile to appear and click in the upper-right corner on ”Edit”.

Then you can add the tile of your newly created form in your preferred section by clicking on the ”+” in the upper-right corner of the section.

In the pop-up, select your tile and add it. After adding the tile, you can change the position via drag and drop. Then save your changes on the site by clicking the ”Save” button in the upper-right corner.

 

Step 5: Grant roles access to the tile

You’ve created a tile for your start form and added it to your preferred site in SAP Build Work Zone. Next, grant role access so users can see the tile. Without this, the tile won’t be visible to anyone.

In ”Content Manager”, open the user role you want to grant access to.

Then click on the upper-right side on ”Edit” and activate the ”Assignment Status” for your tile. Save your changes by clicking on the ”Save” button.

Conclusion

You have now set up an SAP BPA start form as a tile in SAP Build Work Zone. By collecting the required IDs from your workflow, configuring the app parameters and navigation, and assigning the appropriate role access, your users can trigger SAP BPA workflows directly from their Work Zone site with a single click.
This setup works independently of SAP IPD Collaboration, making it a flexible approach for any Work Zone tenant.
If you run into issues or have questions, feel free to leave a comment below.

entry.title: Seamless Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On with the Joule Work mobile app
entry.content:
With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app (formerly known as SAP Mobile Start) introduces a feature that many customers have been asking for: seamless Single Sign-On when opening web applications in the in-app browser. In this post I want to explain the problem this feature solves, how it works conceptually, and what administrators need to configure to enable it.

The Problem: Two Sessions, One App Experience

Joule Work mobile app (formerly known as SAP Mobile Start), when connected to SAP Build Work Zone, is the native mobile entry point for the Intelligent Enterprise. From a single app, users can access native features like widgets, push notifications, and smartwatch complications, but also launch web applications directly from the app – for example SAP Fiori apps from SAP S/4HANA, collaborative Workspaces in SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition, custom extensions build on the SAP Business Technology Platform, and more.

When a user taps on a tile that opens a web application, the Joule Work mobile app instantiates the in-app browser provided by the mobile operating system – iOS or Android. This is a technical context switch: the native application session, which is managed by SAP Mobile Services and backed by SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication, is logically separated from the browser sessions of web applications, for example SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition.

As a result, the in-app browser may not have a valid session for the target web application when launched. The user is redirected to the identity provider and prompted to log in again. This is particularly jarring because, from the user's perspective, they are just navigating within their app.

The Previous Recommendation – and Why It Was Not Ideal for all customers

Customers could avoid this by enabling the "Remember me" functionality of SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication. This feature creates a long-living session cookie in the browser, keeping users signed in across browser sessions for a configurable duration. A similar capability exists on many corporate identity providers (third-party IdPs) to which SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication may be configured delegating the actual authentication. Long-lived cookies on these providers effectively keep the user session alive in the in-app browser in the same manner.

However, this approach has a fundamental drawback: long-lived session cookies are not ideal and may not be wanted to be generically configured on the company's Identity Provider. Furthermore, "Remember me" only prolongs the time until a user is asked to sign-in again in the in-app browser – it is not fully getting rid of it.

Another alternative that some customers consider is password-less sign-in using client certificates. While this is a genuinely secure and, once set up, a convenient approach, it is not feasible for all customers and all users. Client certificates typically require an active Mobile Device Management (MDM) system to push certificates to managed devices – meaning unmanaged devices, common in bring your own device (BYOD) environments, are excluded. Beyond the deployment challenge, mobile operating systems restrict access to the system keychain for third-party apps, so not all in-app browser variants can reliably make use of client certificates. Other password-less options such as FIDO passkeys or biometric-based flows can require some form of manual user interaction during authentication, which breaks the seamless experience users expect when tapping a tile in the app.

The New Solution: Mobile-to-Web SSO

With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app introduces a dedicated Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On flow that bridges the native session and the in-app browser – securely, without requiring long-lived cookies. Here is how it works conceptually:

The mobile app already holds a valid session with SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (in the context of SAP Build Work Zone, to which the app is connected to). The SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant generally serves as the trust anchor for both SAP Build Work Zone, the native app and other SAP and non-SAP solutions including web applications exposed to SAP Build Work Zone. This shared trust is what makes it possible to bridge the two worlds: because both the native app session and the target web applications are anchored in the same SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant, it can act as a secure intermediary, translating the native session into a web session in the in-app browser without requiring the user to authenticate again.

When the user opens a web application in the in-app browser, the app first exchanges its active native session for a short-lived, one-time use SSO token issued by SAP Cloud Identity Services. This token is valid for only a very short time and can only be used once by the mobile app for (re-)establishing a session in the in-app browser on the mobile device.

This is done by passing this one-time SSO token to SAP Cloud Identity Services within the browser context. The SAP Cloud Identity Services validates the token, maps it to the user's identity & native session, and establishes a regular web session – setting the standard browser session cookies. From there, the accessed target web application authenticates against SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication transparently via its standard SAML or OIDC flow reusing the established IAS session with no user interaction required.

The key properties of this approach:

  • Security-friendly: The SSO token is a one-time use token with a short validity. There are no long-lived session cookies involved on the identity provider side.
  • Admin-controlled: The feature can be controlled via a feature flag in SAP Mobile Services Admin Cockpit of the Joule Work mobile app. The administrator also defines exactly which web applications are trusted for this flow by configuring them in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication.
  • Broadly compatible: The feature works with all in-app browser variants offered by Joule Work mobile app on iOS and Android. It also works with all web applications – SAP solutions as well as custom or third-party apps – as long as they share a common SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication tenant with the environment the mobile app is onboarded to.

Key Prerequisite: SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication as the Common Trust Anchor

For the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow to work, SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication must be the identity provider for both the native mobile application and all web applications that should benefit from seamless SSO. The trust is established and maintained through SAP Cloud Identity Services, making it the single common denominator across all participants.

It is important to clarify that SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication does not need to be your end users' primary authentication system. In many enterprise landscapes, SAP Cloud Identity Services acts as an identity proxy, delegating the actual authentication to a corporate identity provider such as Microsoft Entra ID or other identity providers. This is fully supported. The only requirement is that SAP Cloud Identity Services is the first common trust anchor in the chain – meaning all participating web applications are configured to trust it directly, even if it in turn proxies to a corporate IdP.

One configuration detail to be aware of in proxy setups: the "Forward All SSO Requests to Corporate IdP" setting in SAP Cloud Identity Services must not be enabled for the applications involved in the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow. When this setting is active, SAP Cloud Identity Services bypasses its own session handling and always redirects to the corporate IdP – which would cause the Mobile-to-Web SSO token to be ignored, and users would end up with a login prompt again. Beyond Mobile-to-Web SSO, enabling this setting can also cause issues in iFrame-based in-place app integration scenarios, as corporate identity providers frequently block iFraming to protect against clickjacking and similar attack vectors. For more information on this setting, see Forward All SSO Requests to Corporate IdP (SAP Help).

The trust for the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow is configured explicitly in SAP Cloud Identity Services. The administrator defines which web application entries in SAP Cloud Identity Services are allowed to participate in this flow – only applications that have been explicitly added to the trust configuration can benefit from it. In addition, SAP Cloud Identity Services validates the URL of the target web application against the allowed redirect URIs configured for the trusted application. This means a web application can only receive a seamless SSO session if its URL matches one of the permitted redirect URI patterns. Both aspects – the application trust list and the redirect URI validation – ensure that the administrator retains full control over which applications can be reached via this mechanism.

Please note: Customers with SAP Build Work Zone subscriptions created before March 2025 may not yet be using SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication directly. For these environments, it is necessary to explicitly switch the authentication mechanism. Subscriptions created after that date are automatically set up with SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication. Please refer to the SAP Help documentation for the migration steps: Switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication.

Pro tip: As with other features that depend on shared sessions and cookies – such as the iFrame-based in-place integration of web applications – it is important that all components share a common super domain. We strongly recommend operating SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication and your SAP cloud solutions under the SAP Common Super Domain cloud.sap, while other domains work as well as long as they are aligned across the stack to prevent cross-domain restrictions. If you are not yet familiar with this topic, our earlier blog post explains the background and the configuration steps in detail: Demystifying the Common Super Domain for SAP Mobile Start.

Which Web Applications Are Covered Automatically?

When Joule Work mobile app is connected to SAP Build Work Zone, both share the same Application entry in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication. This means that SAP Build Work Zone itself is automatically within the trust scope – no additional configuration is needed for it.

This automatic coverage includes for example:

  • SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition Workspaces – collaborative workspace content embedded in the Work Zone experience
  • Custom extensions hosted on SAP BTP – applications built for example with the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, deployed to the SAP BTP and served using the Approuter of SAP Build Work Zone. (For details on building such extensions, see Building Pro-Code Extensions for SAP Mobile Start)
  • SAP Fiori apps from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition running through an SAP Cloud Connector using a Principal Propagation Destination – these are tunneled through the BTP subaccount and are therefore covered by the same Application entry

The key distinction is that automatic coverage applies to applications that are part of – or tunneled through – the SAP BTP subaccount and its corresponding SAP Cloud Identity Services Application. Applications that run on their own infrastructure and have their own independent trust relationship with SAP Cloud Identity Services are not automatically covered, regardless of whether they are launched in embedded ("in-place") mode or opened directly ("ex-place"). A prominent example is SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: even when it is iFramed within the SAP Build Work Zone shell, it has its own Application entry in SAP Cloud Identity Services and must be explicitly added to the Native-Web Trust group.

For all such web applications – SAP solutions with their own IAS Application entry, third-party apps, or any app not part of the Work Zone BTP subaccount – administrators add them to the trust configuration in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication as described in the setup steps below.

Administrator Setup

Step 1: Verify That IAS-Based Authentication Is Active

Before enabling the feature, confirm that SAP Build Work Zone is using SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication as its identity provider. If this is not yet the case, follow the migration steps referenced in the prerequisite section above.

Step 2: Enable the Feature Flag in SAP Mobile Services

In the SAP Mobile Services Admin Cockpit, navigate to the Client Settings. In the Feature Flags section, locate the entry named "MobiletoWebSingleSign-On" and activate it.

Screenshot showing the highlighted feature flag in the activated state in the Mobile Services Admin Cockpit.

For reference, the corresponding documentation can be found here: Single Sign-On for Web Apps – SAP Help

Step 3: Add Trusted Web Applications in SAP Cloud Identity Services

For web applications that are not automatically covered through the SAP Build Work Zone application (see the previous section), you can add them to the trust configuration:

  1. Sign in to the administration console for SAP Cloud Identity Services.
  2. Go to Applications & Resources → Applications.
  3. Select the application entry that corresponds to your SAP Build Work Zone instance.
  4. Go to the Trust tab, then navigate to Application APIs → Dependencies → Native-Web Trust.
  5. Click Add and select the application with which you want to establish trust.
  6. Save your selection.

Screenshot of SAP Cloud Identity Services to maintain Native-Web Trust Settings

Users opening applications from this trust list in the in-app browser will now benefit from the seamless Mobile-to-Web SSO flow.

Step 4: Verify the Redirect URIs of Trusted Applications

As described in the prerequisite section, SAP Cloud Identity Services validates the URL of the target web application against the allowed redirect URIs of the trusted application entry. For SAP-delivered solutions – such as SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – the redirect URIs are typically pre-configured correctly by SAP and no further action is needed.

However, for custom-built applications or third-party web apps that rely on the general SAP Business Technology Platform application entry in SAP Cloud Identity Services, you should verify that the application's URL is covered by the configured redirect URI patterns. If the URL does not match any permitted pattern, the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow will not complete successfully for that application.

You can review and adjust the redirect URIs in the SAP Cloud Identity Services administration console under the application's configuration. For the rules and wildcard syntax supported for redirect URIs, refer to the SAP Help documentation: Redirect URIs and Post Logout Redirect URI Rules (SAP Help).

Summary

With the Mobile-to-Web SSO feature, Joule Work mobile app version 3.0 closes the session gap between the native app context and the in-app browser – cleanly, securely, and with full administrator control.

Key takeaways:

  • The feature eliminates the need for long-lived "Remember me" cookies on the identity provider, which are a security concern and not universally available.
  • It is based on short-lived, one-time use SSO tokens issued and validated by SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication, which acts as the common trust anchor.
  • The feature is optional and in control by the customer: an administrator (de-/) activates it via a feature flag in SAP Mobile Services Admin Cockpit of Joule Work mobile app and controls which web applications participate via the Native-Web Trust configuration in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication.
  • SAP Build Work Zone web UIs and tunneled embedded business apps are automatically covered. Additional apps can be added with a few clicks.
  • The feature is compatible with all in-app browser variants in Joule Work mobile app and all trusted web applications, regardless of whether they are SAP solutions or custom apps.

If you need further assistance configuring and making use of this new feature in the Joule Work mobile app, feel free to contact our product management team using jouleworkmobile@sap.com, which will be able to suggest the next steps for your scenario.

Further Reading

  • Joule Work mobile app – Single Sign-On for Web Apps (SAP Help)
  • Switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (SAP Help)
  • Demystifying the Common Super Domain for SAP Mobile Start (SAP Community Blog)
  • Building Pro-Code Extensions for SAP Mobile Start – Leveraging Mobile Native Features (SAP Community Blog)
  • SAP Mobile Experience Community Page

entry.title: How to Deploy a Custom Fiori App to S/4HANA Public Cloud and Add a Tile in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
How to Deploy a Custom Fiori App to S/4HANA Public Cloud and Add a Tile in SAP Build Work Zone

Introduction

If you've built a custom Fiori application on SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud using RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) and want to expose it as a tile in SAP Build Work Zone, this tutorial walks you through the complete process — from deployment in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) to the final working tile in Work Zone.
This guide assumes you already have:
  • A working RAP-based OData service on S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • A Fiori application project in BAS that consumes this service
  • Access to SAP Build Work Zone (standard edition)
  • The app accessible via ADT preview

By the end of this tutorial, your app will be accessible as a tile in your Work Zone site, sitting alongside other tiles like My Inbox.

Architecture Overview

SAP Business Application Studio (BAS)
        │
        │  Deploy to ABAP Repository
        ▼
SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 
   ├── OData Service (Service Binding)
   ├── IAM App (External App) + LADI
   ├── Business Catalog
   └── Business Role
        │
        │  Semantic Object + Action
        ▼
SAP Build Work Zone (BTP)
   └── Tile → resolves intent → opens S/4 app via SSO

Prerequisites

  • SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system with developer access
  • SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) with a destination configured to your S/4 system
  • SAP Build Work Zone subscription on your BTP subaccount
  • A working Fiori app project in BAS (currently accessible via ADT preview only)
  • Both BTP and S/4 trusting the same SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) tenant for SSO

Step 1: Configure crossNavigation in manifest.json

Before deploying, your Fiori app needs to declare how it will be launched from the Fiori Launchpad. This is done via the crossNavigation/inbounds section in manifest.json.
Open webapp/manifest.json and add the following inside the "sap.app" section:
"crossNavigation": {
  "inbounds": {
    "intent1": {
      "semanticObject": "ZObjectName",
      "action": "display",
      "signature": {
        "parameters": {},
        "additionalParameters": "allowed"
      },
      "title": "App Title",
      "subTitle": "SAP Fiori application",
      "icon": "sap-icon://icon_name"
    }
  }
}
Key points:
  • The semanticObject and action together form the intent (#ZObjectName-display) that the Fiori Launchpad and Work Zone will use to navigate to your app.
  • Choose a meaningful semantic object name (prefix with Z for custom).
  • The title here will appear on the tile.
  • To browse available icons for your tile, see the Icon Explorer . 
Important: Also ensure your i18n property in sap.app uses the simple string format:
"i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties"
Do NOT use the object format: { "bundleName": "..." }

Note: The S/4HANA Cloud LADI converter cannot process the object format for i18n. Use the simple string format instead. For more details on this issue, see:
UI5 CLI - GitHub Issue #432 
SAP Help Portal | SAP Online Help 

Your manifest.json should look like: 

 

Step 2: Ensure i18n.properties File Exists

Make sure the file webapp/i18n/i18n.properties exists and contains at minimum:
appTitle=App Title
appDescription=SAP Fiori Application
Without this file, the LADI creation will fail with the error: "File is not a 'properties' file for texts."

Step 3: Configure Deployment to ABAP

In BAS, configure the deployment target. There are two ways to do this:
Option A: Via Application Info (UI-based)
  1. Right-click your project and select "Open Application Info"
  2. Click the "Deploy" option
  3. Fill in the deployment configuration fields
Option B: Via terminal command
npm run deploy-config
This launches the same configuration wizard in the terminal.
 
Both approaches will prompt you to fill in the following fields:

FieldValue
Target TypeABAP
DestinationYour S/4 destination 
SAPUI5 ABAP RepositoryA unique name, max 15 chars (eg: ZREPO_NAME)
Deployment DescriptionAny suitable description 
PackageYour ABAP package
Transport RequestYour transport request (or leave empty for $TMP)

This generates a ui5-deploy.yaml file:

Step 4: Deploy the Application

Run the deployment from the BAS terminal:
npm run deploy
This builds the app and deploys it to the S/4HANA Cloud ABAP repository.

What to look for in the output:

SAPUI5 Application has been uploaded and registered successfully
Updated LADIs for UI5 Repository "", package "", transport ""
Inbound intent1 (ZObjectName-display) was converted to LADI ZREPO_NAME_UI5R
Launchpad App Descriptor Item ZREPO_NAME_UI5R was updated
Deployment Successful.

For example:

 

If you see warnings like:
  • "No LADI was created as no inbound is defined in manifest.json" → You're missing the crossNavigation block
  • "Could not convert UI5 Descriptor to LADI(s) / File is not a 'properties' file for texts" → Change i18n to the simple string format

Step 5: Create an IAM App (External App) in S/4

After deployment, the LADI exists on the system but isn't yet linked to any access control. You need to create a new IAM App to hold it and grant access to the OData service.
  1. Open your S/4HANA Cloud system
  2. Search for and open the "Create IAM App" application
  3. Create a new IAM App with:
    • App Type: External App
    • IAM App ID: A meaningful name 
  4. In the "Services" section:
    • Add your OData service
    • This grants the app authorization to call the backend service
  5. In the "Fiori Launchpad App Descr Items" section:
    • Add the LADI ID: ZREPO_NAME_UI5R
    • This enables Launchpad intent navigation
  6. Save and publish

 

 

Why a separate IAM App?
Your OData service already has its own IAM App (type: Custom Inbound Service App) for API access. The External App IAM App is specifically for Launchpad navigation.

Step 6: Add IAM App to a Business Catalog

  • Open Maintain Business Catalogs (or create a new catalog in ADT)
  • Open or create your catalog 
  • Add your new External App IAM App to this catalog
  • Publish the catalog

 

 

Step 7: Assign Business Catalog to a Business Role

  • Open Maintain Business Roles
  • Open the Business Role assigned to your users
  • Go to Assigned Business Catalogs
  • Add your catalog 
  • Save

Step 8: Verify on S/4 Fiori Launchpad

Before adding the tile in Work Zone, verify the app works natively on S/4:
https://<your-system>.lab.s4hana.cloud.sap/ui#ZObjectName-display
Your app should open without errors.
If you get "navigation target could not be resolved", double-check:
  • The LADI is added to the IAM App
  • The IAM App is in a Business Catalog
  • The Business Catalog is in your user's Business Role
  • You've logged out and back in after making changes

Step 9: Add the Tile in SAP Build Work Zone

Now that the app works on S/4 via intent navigation, add it to Work Zone:
  • Open SAP Build Work Zone administration
  • Go to Content Manager
  • Create a new App
In the Navigation tab:
  • Semantic Object: ZObjectName (same as the one in manifest.json)
  • Action: display
In the Visualization tab:
  • Title, subtitle, icon as desired
  • Save the app

Step 10: Assign to Role and Group

  • In Content Manager → Roles: Open your role and assign the new app
  • In Content Manager → Groups: Open your group (e.g., "SBPA") and add the app
  • Ensure the role is mapped to a Role Collection that your users have

Step 11: Verify the Tile in Work Zone

Open your Work Zone site.
Your new tile should appear in the designated group/section.
Click it — the app opens seamlessly via SSO (since both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant).

Troubleshooting

Issue CauseFix
"No LADI was created" during deployMissing crossNavigation/inbounds in manifest.jsonAdd the crossNavigation block and redeploy
"Could not convert UI5 Descriptor to LADI(s)"i18n uses object formatChange to "i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties"
"Navigation target could not be resolved"LADI not linked to IAM App, or missing catalog/role assignmentAdd LADI to External App IAM App → Business Catalog → Business Role
403 Forbidden / UCON blockedTrying to access direct BSP URLUse Launchpad URL (/ui#SemanticObject-action)
Login prompt (username/password) in Work ZoneURL uses -api endpoint, or SSO not configuredUse non-api URL; ensure both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant
"Destination https://https://..." errorDouble protocol in URLRemove the extra https:// prefix

Key Takeaways

  • crossNavigation/inbounds in manifest.json is what creates the LADI during deployment.
  • i18n must use the simple string format ("i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties") for the LADI converter to work.
  • A separate IAM App of type External App is needed for the Launchpad tile.
  • The chain must be complete: LADI → IAM App → Business Catalog → Business Role → User
  • In Work Zone, use semantic object + action — not URL tiles.
  • SSO works automatically when both BTP and S/4 trust the same IAS tenant.

Conclusion

The path from "app works in ADT preview" to "tile in Work Zone" requires a clear understanding of the deployment chain:
  • BAS deploys the UI and creates the LADI
  • The IAM App links the LADI to access control
  • Work Zone resolves the semantic intent
Each piece is simple on its own, but missing any single step results in confusing errors.
Once this flow is established, adding future apps follows the same pattern — making it repeatable and scalable across your organization.

Tags

SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Business Application Studio, Fiori, RAP, ABAP Cloud, LADI, IAM App, Tile Configuration

entry.title: End to End Guide: Enabling SAP Joule in SAP S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad (PCE + OnPremise PoC)
entry.content:
1. Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP's generative AI, designed to help users accomplish tasks through natural language interaction within SAP applications. This guide will provide a step by step walkthrough to enable joule in SAP Fiori.

The guide is primarily written for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition (PCE).
PS: Also covers steps that differ for On-Premise S/4HANA (for a Proof-of-Concept environment).

2. Component Overview

Before starting, it is important to understand the key components involved and the role each plays.

Component

What It Is

Role in This Integration

SAP Joule

SAP's generative AI copilot. A cloud service on SAP BTP.

The AI assistant end users interact with. Loaded in FLP header as a Shell Plugin.

SAP BTP

SAP Business Technology Platform  the cloud foundation.

Hosts Joule, Work Zone, Identity Services, and destinations connecting to S/4HANA.

SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

SAP's digital workplace platform. Can serve as launchpad or content bridge.

Acts as the technical bridge between Joule and S/4HANA. Required in the Joule Formation.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

SAP's Identity Authentication Service. Manages user identity and SSO.

Provides the SSO layer. All BTP services require IAS authentication.

SAP Cloud Connector (SCC)

An on-premise agent creating a secure tunnel from BTP to on-premise systems.

Enables Work Zone and Joule on BTP to securely reach S/4HANA.

SAP Fiori Launchpad (FLP)

The S/4HANA web UI hub where users launch Fiori apps.

The surface where the Joule icon appears via a Shell Plugin target mapping.

Formation

A BTP System Landscape construct grouping systems for a specific integration.

Tells Joule which Work Zone, IAS tenant, and S/4HANA belong together.

CDM3

Common Data Model v3 . SAP Fiori's content model (catalogs, tiles, groups).

Used to expose S/4HANA Fiori content to BTP so Work Zone can sync it.

3. Architecture Overview

The following describes the end-to-end integration flow:

  • End User opens S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad in browser
  • FLP authenticates via IAS (after SSO is configured)
  • FLP loads the Joule Shell Plugin from the Joule Web Client (sapdas.cloud.sap)
  • Joule on BTP connects to Work Zone via the Joule Formation
  • Work Zone reaches S/4HANA via BTP Destinations → Cloud Connector → ABAP Server(S/4 system)
  • Joule icon appears in FLP header. Users interact with Joule through natural language.

4. Prerequisites

Requirement

Details

SAP BTP Global Account

Admin access. Subaccount in the same region as your IAS tenant.

SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)

IAS tenant associated with your BTP Global Account.

SAP S/4HANA System

PCE or On-Premise (for PoC). FLP accessible via browser.

SAP Cloud Connector

Installed and running. Registered to your BTP subaccount.

S/4HANA Technical User

User with access to CDM3 service (/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3) and FLP admin roles.

BTP Entitlements

Joule (foundation), SAP Build Work Zone standard edition, SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Regional Alignment

BTP subaccount, IAS tenant, and Work Zone must be in the same region.

Note: PCE vs On-Premise: PCE uses token-based registration via Communication Arrangements. On-Premise uses Cloud Connector and manual destinations. Each step calls out differences.

5. Step-by-Step Guide

Step 1: Enable Cloud Foundry in BTP Subaccount

Why: Cloud Foundry is the runtime where BTP service instances (Work Zone, Joule) are deployed. Without it, subscriptions and the Joule booster cannot run.

  1. Open SAP BTP Cockpit → navigate to your target Subaccount → Overview.
  2. Click Enable Cloud Foundry. Accept defaults (Plan: standard). Click Create.
  3. Once enabled, click Create Space → name it e.g. 'dev'. Assign yourself Space Developer + Space Manager roles.

Validation: Subaccount Overview shows CF Enabled, Org Name, API Endpoint, and space 'dev' listed.

On-Premise PoC: No difference. This step is identical for both PCE and On-Premise.

 

Step 2: Add Entitlements

Why: Entitlements grant your subaccount the right to use specific BTP services. Without them, the services cannot be subscribed to or instantiated.

  1. Go to Global Account → Entitlements → Entity Assignments.
  2. Select your subaccount → Configure Entitlements → Add Service Plans.
  3. Add: Joule (also search as 'das-application') — plan: foundation
  4. Add: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition — plan: standard
  5. Add: SAP Cloud Identity Services — plan: connectivity
  6. Click Save.

Validation: All three services appear in Entitlements list with quota assigned.

Step 3: Establish Trust to IAS

Why: All BTP services (including Joule) authenticate via IAS. Establishing trust allows your subaccount to delegate authentication to IAS, enabling SSO across all connected services.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Trust Configuration.
  2. Click Establish Trust.
  3. Select your IAS tenant (e.g., yourtenantid.accounts.ondemand.com).
  4. Click Next through the wizard → Finish.

Validation: Trust Configuration shows your IAS tenant with status Active.

Step 4: Configure Trusted Domains in BTP

Why: BTP Trusted Domains control which browser origins can interact with your subaccount's services via iFrames and cross-origin calls. Without this, the Joule panel cannot be embedded in your FLP.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Security → Settings → Trusted Domains.
  2. Click Add for each of the following origins (scheme + host + port if non-standard):
  • Your FLP origin: e.g. https://your-s4-host:44301
  • Your Joule Web Client: https://<tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap
  • Your Work Zone site: https://<site-id>.launchpad.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
  1. Click Save.

Note: Your Joule URL is found in System Landscape → Formations → your Joule formation → URL column of the Joule system entry.(You can maintain it later once Joule is activated via booster etc.)

Validation: All required origins appear in the Trusted Domains list.

Step 5: Configure Trusted Domains in IAS

Why: IAS maintains its own trusted domain allowlist. Cross-domain authentication requires IAS to trust the same origins as BTP.

  1. Open IAS Admin Console: https://<ias-tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/admin
  2. Go to Applications & Resources → Tenant Settings → Customization → Trusted Domains.
  3. Click Add — enter hostname only (no scheme or port) for each host.
  4. Click Save.

Note: Format difference: BTP accepts full origins (https://host:44301). IAS accepts hostname only (host.example.com).

Validation: IAS shows 'Trusted Domains updated' confirmation.

Step 6: Expose SAP Fiori Launchpad Content (CDM3)

Why: Work Zone needs to know what Fiori apps and catalogs exist in S/4HANA. The CDM3 export makes this content discoverable so Work Zone can sync it.

Pre-requisite: Activate CDM3 ICF Service

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction SICF.
  2. Navigate to /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3. If inactive, right-click → Activate Service.

Expose Content

  1. Run transaction /n/UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE.
  2. Select Version 1.
  3. Choose All roles (first-time setup) or select specific roles.
  4. Click Expose Content in the toolbar. Wait for completion.
  5. Click View Log to verify results.

Note: Warnings in the CDM3 log are normal. Red errors should be investigated but typically do not block the integration.

Validation: Log shows entities exposed (e.g., '14,663 out of 14,671 entities exposed'). Click View Exposed Content to confirm.

Step 7: Set Up SAP Cloud Connector

Why: Cloud Connector creates a secure outbound-only tunnel from your on-premise/PCE network to BTP. Work Zone and Joule use this tunnel to reach S/4HANA without inbound firewall rules.

Please setup cloud connector first.

7.1 Register BTP Subaccount

  1. Open SCC Admin UI (e.g. https://localhost:8443).
  2. Add your BTP subaccount using Download Authentication Data from BTP → Connectivity → Cloud Connectors.
  3. Confirm status shows Connected (green).

7.2 Create Back-End Mapping

  1. Go to Cloud to On-Premises → + Add:
  • Protocol: HTTPS (preferred) or HTTP (PoC)
  • Internal Host/Port: e.g. S/4HANA internal hostname + HTTPS port 44301 or HTTP port 8001
  • Virtual Host: a clean alias (e.g., s4hana-virtual)
  • Back-End Type: ABAP System
  1. Add Resources: /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3, /sap/bc/ui2/flp, /sap/bc/ui5_ui5 — all with 'Path and All Sub-Paths'.

Validation: Mapping shows green 'Reachable' status. BTP Cockpit → Connectivity → Cloud Connectors shows 'Available'.

On-Premise PoC: If HTTPS port 44301 is blocked between SCC and ABAP server, use HTTP port 8001 for PoC. In SICF, navigate to /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3 → Logon Data tab → check 'Use all logon procedures' to allow Basic Authentication.

Step 8: Activate Subscriptions and Instances

Why: Subscriptions activate the actual services in your subaccount. Without subscribing to Work Zone and Joule, neither the Joule icon nor the content bridge can function.

  1. Go to BTP Subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → Create.
  2. Subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition — plan: standard.
  3. Subscribe to Joule (das-application) — plan: foundation.
  4. For Work Zone: also create a Service Instance (name: BuildZone) and a Service Key.
  5. Launch Work Zone from Instances & Subscriptions at least once — this registers it in System Landscape.

Note: The Joule Booster (next step) looks for the Work Zone subscription in its dropdown. It only appears after you launch Work Zone at least once.

Validation: Subscriptions show 'Subscribed'. Service Key created. System Landscape → Systems shows Work Zone auto-registered.

Step 9: Configure the Joule Formation

Why: A Formation is a logical grouping that tells Joule which Work Zone, IAS tenant, and S/4HANA belong together. Without a formation, Joule has no context about which systems to connect to.

Option A — Run the Joule Booster (Recommended for PCE)

  1. Go to Global Account → Boosters → search 'Setting Up Joule' → click Start.
  2. Wizard: Select subaccount and CF space (dev).
  3. Step 3 — Select Integrations: choose SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.
  4. Step 4 — Select Capabilities: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition + SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.
  5. Step 5 — Select your Work Zone system and S/4HANA system.
  6. Click Execute and wait for completion.

Option B — Add Systems to Existing Formation

  1. Go to Global Account → System Landscape → Formations.
  2. Find the formation of type 'Integration with Joule' with status Ready.
  3. Click Include Systems → add your Work Zone instance and S/4HANA system.
  4. Confirm integration options (Enable Capability Deployment, Enable Joule Icon).

Validation: Formation shows status Ready (green). Includes IAS, Work Zone, and Joule systems. Note the Joule URL — needed for Trusted Domains and shell plugin.

On-Premise PoC: On-Premise systems cannot complete Communication Arrangement token registration.

Recommended approach:

(1) Run the booster selecting only Work Zone as the integration (not S/4HANA).
(2) Register S/4HANA manually in System Landscape → Systems → Add → Type: SAP S/4HANA Cloud. (Not needed for POC)

Step 10: Configure BTP Destinations

Why: BTP Destinations define how Work Zone and Joule connect to S/4HANA. Without correct destinations, Work Zone cannot read content and Joule cannot reach the backend.

Create the following destination (Design-Time and Runtime can be the same for PoC):

Property

Value

Name

e.g, jouletc  (lowercase, no underscores)

Type

HTTP

URL

http://<scc-virtual-host>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3

Proxy Type

OnPremise

Authentication

BasicAuthentication

User / Password

S/4HANA technical user with CDM3 access

sap-client

Your S/4HANA client (e.g., 100)

HTML5.DynamicDestination

true

WebIDEEnabled

true

WebIDEUsage

odata_abap

 

Critical: Work Zone Destination Naming: Work Zone only accepts destination names that are lowercase with no underscores. Names like MCF_TC will cause an error. Use names like jouletc or s4hana.

Critical: URL Path: The destination URL must include the CDM3 path: /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3. Without this, Work Zone receives an HTML login page instead of JSON, resulting in an authorization error.
