Validation: Check Connection returns HTTP 200. e.g. SWZ_JOULE destination is auto-created and visible in the destinations list.

 

Step 11: Configure Work Zone Channel Manager

Why: The Channel Manager connects Work Zone to S/4HANA as a Content Provider. This sync imports S/4HANA's Fiori catalogs and makes the app landscape available to Joule.

  1. Launch SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition from Instances & Subscriptions.
  2. Go to Channel Manager → + New.
  3. Fill in:
  • Title: your system name (e.g., S4H_UIB)
  • Design-Time Destination: your lowercase destination (e.g., jouletc)
  • Runtime Destination: same
  • Content Scope: Roles and related content
  • Automatically add all content items: ON
  1. Click Save, then click the sync icon.

Wait 5-15 minutes for first sync

On-Premise PoC: The channel may show 'Updated' with a 'Connectivity Error' in the Report. This error is for real-time change notification callbacks, which on-premise systems do not support. The content sync itself succeeds. Re-sync manually when S/4HANA content changes.

Validation: Channel shows Updated status. Content Manager → Explore confirms S/4HANA catalogs are visible.

Step 12: Configure Joule Shell Plugin in S/4HANA FLP

Why: The Joule icon in FLP is delivered via a SAPUI5 Shell Plugin configured as a Target Mapping in an FLP catalog. Without this step, users will not see the Joule icon regardless of BTP configuration.

12.1 Create Technical Catalog

  1. In S/4HANA, run transaction /n/UI2/FLPD_CUST (Launchpad Content Manager: Cross-Client).
  2. In the Catalogs tab, create a new catalog: ID = Z_JOULE_TECH, Title = 'Joule Technical Catalog'. Package: $TMP (PoC).

12.2 Create Shell Plugin Target Mapping

  1. Open catalog Z_JOULE_TECH in the Launchpad Designer (opens in browser).
  2. Click Target Map... tab → Create Target Mapping.
  3. Fill in:
  • Semantic Object: Shell
  • Action: plugin  (type manually — not in dropdown)
  • Application Type: SAPUI5 Fiori App
  • URL: https://<joule-tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/resources/public/webclient/s4  (NO trailing slash)
  • Title: Joule
  • ID: sap.das.webclientplugin.s4
  • Save.

Critical: No Trailing Slash: The Joule Web Client URL must NOT end with a slash. Use .../webclient/s4 not .../webclient/s4/. A trailing slash causes 'File not found' and the icon will not appear.

Note: How to find your Joule URL: Go to System Landscape → Formations → your Joule formation. The Joule system shows a URL like https://<tenant>.eu10.sapdas.cloud.sap. Append /resources/public/webclient/s4 to this base URL.

12.3 Create Role and Assign Catalog

  1. Run transaction PFCG → create single role Z_JOULE_S4HANA.
  2. Menu tab → click Launchpad Catalog → assign Z_JOULE_TECH (Local Front-End Server).
  3. Authorizations tab → Change Authorization Data → Do not select templates → Generate → Save.
  4. User tab → assign role to target users → User Comparison → Full Comparison → OK.

Validation: Log in to FLP. The Joule icon (sparkle/diamond) appears in the FLP header. Clicking it opens the Joule panel.

Step 13: Configure IAS SSO for S/4HANA FLP (Recommended)

Why: Joule is secured by IAS. If FLP uses ABAP local login, users must log in again when Joule opens. Configuring IAS SSO for FLP gives a seamless single login experience.

13.1 Download IAS Metadata

  1. Open IAS Admin Console → Applications & Resources → Tenant Settings → SAML 2.0 Configuration.
  2. Click Download Metadata File. Save the XML.

13.2 Configure SAML2 in S/4HANA

  1. Run transaction SAML2 in S/4HANA.
  2. Enable SAML 2.0 and create a Local Provider Name.
  3. Trusted Providers tab → Add → Upload Metadata File → upload IAS metadata.
  4. Set NameID format (Login Name recommended — maps to ABAP user ID).
  5. Download S/4HANA SP metadata.

13.3 Register S/4HANA as Application in IAS

  1. IAS Admin Console → Applications → + Add.
  2. Upload S/4HANA SP metadata under Trust → SAML 2.0 Configuration.
  3. Set Subject Name Identifier to match your ABAP user attribute (Login Name).
  4. Save.

Validation: Opening FLP URL redirects to IAS login. After login, FLP opens without ABAP credential prompts. Joule opens without a separate login.

6. Troubleshooting

Symptom

Likely Cause

Fix

Joule icon not visible in FLP

Shell plugin not loaded or role not assigned

Verify role Z_JOULE_S4HANA is assigned. Hard-reload FLP (Ctrl+Shift+R). Check browser console for plugin errors.

'File not found' when Joule loads

Trailing slash in plugin URL

Remove trailing slash: use .../webclient/s4 not .../webclient/s4/.

'Refused to frame/connect' error

Missing Trusted Domain entry

Add the exact blocked origin (shown in browser console) to BTP and IAS Trusted Domains.

Work Zone Channel: Connectivity Error

Real-time callbacks not supported (on-premise)

If status shows 'Updated' with Connectivity Error — content synced. Re-sync manually as needed.

Work Zone 'authorization error' on fetch

Destination URL missing CDM3 path

Ensure destination URL is http://<host>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3.

Booster fails: 'System already part of formation'

IAS can only be in one Joule formation

Find the existing Joule formation and add your systems to it instead.

SCC mapping 'Not Reachable'

Firewall blocking the port

Ask network team to open the port between SCC and ABAP server. Try internal IP instead of hostname.

Joule opens but prompts for login

IAS SSO not configured for FLP

Complete Step 13 — configure SAML2 trust between S/4HANA and IAS.

Work Zone destination name error

Name has uppercase or underscores

Rename destination to lowercase with no underscores (e.g., jouletc).

Wrong Joule URL after S/4HANA 2025 upgrade

DAS_WEBCLIENT_PLUGIN set to pilot URL

Update /UI2/FLP_SYS_CONF (SM30) with your tenant URL, or remove the parameter.

7. Summary — What We Achieved

  • Joule provisioned on SAP BTP and linked to IAS and Work Zone via a Formation
  • SAP Cloud Connector established as the secure bridge between BTP and S/4HANA
  • Work Zone Channel Manager syncing S/4HANA Fiori catalogs to BTP
  • BTP Destinations correctly routing Work Zone to S/4HANA via Cloud Connector
  • Joule Shell Plugin configured in S/4HANA FLP via a Technical Catalog and PFCG role
  • Joule icon visible and functional in the S/4HANA Fiori Launchpad

entry.title: SAP Build Work Zone + SAP Service Cloud V2: Creating a True Single Entry Point for Customer Service
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's enterprise landscape, employees often need to navigate multiple applications to complete a single business process. Service agents switch between CRM systems, ERP applications, workflow tools, and custom applications throughout the day, resulting in fragmented user experiences and reduced productivity.

The vision of a Unified Digital Workplace is simple: provide users with a single entry point where they can access all relevant applications, data, workflows, and insights based on their role.

SAP Build Work Zone has become the strategic digital workplace solution for SAP customers. Organizations already leverage Work Zone as a central access point for solutions such as SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, and custom SAP BTP applications.

Now, the same experience can be extended to customer-facing teams by integrating SAP Service Cloud Version 2 (V2) into SAP Build Work Zone, enabling service agents and managers to work from a single, personalized workspace.

In this blog, we will explore the business value, architecture, implementation approach, and a demo scenario showcasing how SAP Build Work Zone and SAP Service Cloud V2 can work together seamlessly.

A Short Story

Meet Prasad, a Customer Service Manager at a global manufacturing company running SAP's full suite.

Every morning, Prasad logs into SAP Build Work Zone. On her home page, she sees her personalized tiles: open production orders from S/4HANA, pending approvals from SuccessFactors, and her team's KPI dashboard. Everything is in one place.

But then her phone rings. A key customer is escalating a service ticket — a machine breakdown at their site. Prasad switches to a completely different browser tab, logs into SAP Service Cloud V2 separately, searches for the customer, finds the ticket, checks the service history, and tries to correlate it with the spare parts availability from S/4HANA — which means another tab, another login.

Three systems. Three tabs. One frustrated manager. One unhappy customer.

This is the problem SAP Build Work Zone + SAP Service Cloud V2 integration solves.

After the integration, Prasad's Work Zone home page now has a dedicated Service Operations section. The Service Cloud V2 app is embedded as a tile and a full sidebar integration. She can:

  • See real-time ticket queue directly from the Work Zone homepage
  • Open and update service tickets without leaving Work Zone
  • WorkZone can Receive intelligent notifications when ticket SLA thresholds are breached

Her morning is now seamless. Her customer gets faster resolution. Her team is more productive.

That is the power of a single entry point.

Key Benefits

True Single Entry Point for All Personas

Persona                                                             Before                                                                          After

Service Agent

2 systems (Work Zone + Service Cloud)

1 — Work Zone

Service Manager

3 systems (Work Zone + Service Cloud + S/4HANA)

1 — Work Zone

IT Admin

Manage 3 separate portals

Manage 1 unified portal

Executive

Multiple dashboards

1 personalized command center

Key Benefits

  • Unified User Experience:  Access SAP Service Cloud V2, SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, and custom applications through one portal.
  • Single Sign-On :Reduce authentication complexity and improve user productivity.
  • Role-Based Access: Deliver personalized content, applications, and workflows based on user roles.
  • Improved Productivity: Minimize context switching between applications and browser tabs.
  • Extensibility: Integrate custom SAPUI5 and SAP BTP applications alongside standard SAP solutions.

Why bring Service Cloud V2 into Work Zone

 

How to surface SAP Service Cloud V2 in Work Zone

  • Subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone, standard or advanced edition

In your BTP subaccount, go to Service Marketplace and subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone. Advanced Edition is required if you need Digital Workplace Service features (content federation, external solutions). Standard is sufficient for most SAP Cloud app integrations.

 
Navigation path: BTP Cockpit → Subaccount → Service Marketplace → SAP Build Work Zone → Create subscription → choose edition → Assign role collections Launchpad_Admin and Launchpad_User to relevant users.

  • Create a destination for SAP Service Cloud V2

Work Zone discovers external SaaS apps through BTP Destinations. SAP Service Cloud V2 exposes OData and REST APIs under its tenant URL. Create a destination with OAuth2 client credentials so Work Zone can fetch app descriptors and launch URLs securely.

Destination config (key fields)

Name: SCV2
Type: HTTP · URL: https://<your-tenant>.crm.cloud.sap
Auth: OAuth2ClientCredentials
Token Service URL: IAS token endpoint
Additional Properties: HTML5.DynamicDestination = true, sap-platform = cf

  • Add Service Cloud V2 as a content provider in Work Zone

Now tell Work Zone where to find those deployed apps. Open the Work Zone Site Manager and register Service Cloud V2 as a content provider. Point it at the HTML5 App Repository app and the SCV2 destination. Work Zone will fetch the CDM and expose the tiles to administrators.

Navigation path

Work Zone Site Manager → Channel Manager → Add → SAP BTP → Content Provider Name: SAP Service Cloud V2 → Runtime Destination: SCV2 → Design-Time Destination: SCV2 → Fetch content → Save.

  • Assign Service Cloud tiles to roles and groups

After fetching content, the Service Cloud V2 tiles appear in the Content Manager under "Available Content". Assign them to a role (e.g., ServiceAgent) and add that role to a launchpad group. 

Navigation path : Work Zone Content Manager → Add to Role → Create/select role ServiceAgent → Assign group Service Cloud Apps → Publish site.
 

See it in action — one launchpad, everything connected

To demonstrate the integration capabilities of SAP Build Work Zone, i created a simple demo showcasing how different applications can be brought together through a unified user experience.

In this video , users can observe the following:

  • Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 Integration
    SAP Build Work Zone provides direct access to Sales and Service Cloud V2, allowing users to launch these applications seamlessly from a single location.
  • Access to Business Objects
    Users can navigate to important business objects such as Accounts and Cases directly from Work Zone, reducing the need to switch between multiple systems.
  • Custom Application Integration
    Custom applications, including SAPUI5 applications deployed on SAP BTP, can be embedded within Work Zone and accessed alongside standard SAP applications.
  • Cross-Application Navigation
    The same custom application embedded in Work Zone can also be accessed from Service Cloud V2, enabling a connected and consistent user journey across platforms.

Why This Matters

By bringing together SAP applications and custom solutions, SAP Build Work Zone becomes a true single entry point for users. Whether users need to access Service Cloud V2, Sales Cloud, business data, or custom applications, everything is available through a unified digital workplace.

This approach simplifies navigation, improves productivity, and delivers a more seamless user experience by reducing the need to manage multiple entry points and disconnected systems.

Conclusion

Organizations often struggle with users navigating across multiple applications and portals. With SAP Build Work Zone, businesses can create a centralized experience that connects SAP solutions and custom applications under one roof. The result is a more intuitive, efficient, and user-friendly workspace that helps employees focus on their work rather than searching for the right application.

entry.title: Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
entry.content:
SAP Joule adoption is booming across SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Digital Supply Chain, SAP Ariba, SAP Concur, and beyond. As more teams activate Joule, configuration and integration challenges naturally emerge — whether you're just getting started or optimizing an existing setup. 

We created this guide to make troubleshooting easier. Based on real-world experience with Joule customers, it offers a straightforward, step-by-step approach to help administrators quickly identify and resolve issues. You'll find practical solutions whether you're tackling an activation challenge or addressing a business user concern. 

Our aim is simple: help you solve problems faster and work more effectively with SAP Support when you need it. Let's get started.  

This blog is part of the Joule Activation series: 

  • SAP Business AI – Overview for all!!!
  • Joule - Unified Setup: Bridging Simplicity and Performance
  • Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - A Comprehensive Setup Guide
  • Joule – End-to-End Setup Guide (for all Line of Business) Unified Approach
  • Managing Joule on SAP BTP: Roles, Analytics, Administration and Troubleshooting
  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule health checks
  • Common configuration issues: Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
  • SAP Joule – Common Configuration Issues 

This blog is based on supporting customer managed Joule implementations, and this blog is part1. The below series of the blogs are core RIG content on Joule Administration and we plan on keeping them up to date (such as with relevant information for SAP Managed Joule implementations in future),  which includes:

  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4

Understanding the Joule Architecture for Issue Resolution Layers

This diagram shows the simplified runtime workflow for SAP Joule, illustrating how users interact with SAP Build Work Zone on SAP BTP and connect to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition through Cloud Connector, with authentication handled by SAP Cloud Identity Services. 

Before diving into troubleshooting, it is essential to understand how Joule operates across multiple architectural layers, let me make it vertical for better understanding. Each layer is a potential critical touchpoint and understanding the stack helps isolate issues quickly. 

How Joule processes a request at runtime: 

  1. The user interacts with the Joule client embedded in an SAP application (Layer 1).
  2. Joule authenticates via SAP Cloud Identity Services (Layer 2) and receives an OpenID Connect (OIDC) token. 
  3. Joule queries the SAP Build Work Zone Navigation Service (Layer 3) to determine which capabilities the user has access to, based on role-content mappings. 
  4. Joule's orchestration evaluates the user prompt against the Scenario Catalog, Knowledge Catalog, and user context (Layer 4). 
  5. If a transactional/navigational capability is matched, Joule calls the backend system via SAP BTP Destination Service and SAP Cloud Connector (Layer 5) using Principal Propagation. 
  6. The backend (Layer 6) processes the request and returns data based on the user's authorizations. 
  7. Joule returns the response to the user. 

Understanding this flow is the key to effective troubleshooting.

We are moving on part2 for the issue resolution workflow.

entry.title: Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
entry.content:
The Recommended Issue Resolution Workflow

This blog is part2. The below series of the blogs are core RIG content on Joule Administration and we plan on keeping them up to date (such as with relevant information for SAP Managed Joule implementations in future),  which includes:

  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4

The following workflow provides a systematic approach to resolving Joule issues. It is divided into two phases: Initial triage (performed by the user or first-level support) and Diagnostic Investigation (performed by the Joule administrator).

Phase 1: Initial Triage

Step 1: Identify the Symptom

Before diving into troubleshooting, take a moment to classify the issue. The fastest path to a fix starts with asking: what kind of problem is it? Match the reported symptom to one of these common categories:

Symptom (Most Common)

Likely Category

Joule icon not visible

UX / Icon Issue

Blank/white screen after clicking icon

SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication (IAS) / Identity Provisioning (IPS)

"Refused to connect" error

Trusted Domains / Connectivity

"Digital Assistant not found"

Formation / SAP BTP Provisioning

Joule only provides SAP Help documentation

Content / Roles / Capabilities

"I'm having trouble connecting"

Connectivity / Principal Propagation

Joule not greeting with user's name

Identity / Global User ID

Booster execution fails

Joule Booster / Activation

Step 2: Download Traces

A quick but important step before escalating any Joule issue: collect your diagnostic data upfront. Fortunately, Joule makes this straightforward. Head to Settings > About > Download Traces, decide whether to include message history, and save the .zip file. This single package contains a wealth of troubleshooting information, including: Joule version and environment details, UI logs and recent HTTP requests, Correlation IDs for the last 5-10 messages, Message history (if consented).

Step 3: Determine the Response Type and Classify the Issue Layer

Per SAP Note 3679363 How to recognize a Joule response's type?, Joule has three response types:

Response Type

How to Recognize

Meaning

Direct Response

Generic content, exclamation mark on hover (Response Insights)

Joule could not match any capability or knowledge source

Information Retrieval

Response includes SAP Help documentation links or custom document sources

Joule matched knowledge content but not a transactional capability

Transactional / Navigational

Response returns business data or app navigation buttons

Capability was successfully selected and executed

A mismatch between the expected and actual response type is a telltale sign of a deeper issue. If you're expecting a transactional or navigational response but Joule returns an informational or direct answer instead, this points to a content, role, or configuration problem that warrants a closer look by your administrator.

Tips:

Joule Response Types (SAP Note 3679363 How to recognize a Joule response's type?)

Direct Response: Generic "I cannot..." + exclamation icon on hover Information Retrieval: Response includes SAP Help doc links Transactional/Navigational: Returns business data or app button

If expected Transactional but got Informational/Direct: -> Follow Path D below (Content/Roles)

The fastest way to get to the bottom of a Joule issue is to match the symptom to the right architecture layer. Use the table from the github repository ([Github] leee03/SAP_Joule_Issue_Catalog) as your starting point — it maps common symptoms to the layer most likely responsible, so you know exactly where to focus your investigation, a simplified view as below:

Symptom

Diagnostic Path

Architecture Layer

Joule icon not visible in SAP Fiori Launchpad

Path F: UX / Icon Appearance

Layer 1: Client/UX

"Refused to connect" / "I'm having trouble connecting"

Path E: Connectivity / Principal Propagation

Layer 5: Connectivity

Joule returns SAP Help content / generic instructions only

Path D : Content Exposure & Roles

Layer 3: SAP Build Work Zone + Layer 6: Backend

"Digital Assistant not found"

 Path E: Connectivity / (Formation)

 Layer 5: Connectivity

Joule not greeting with user name / blank screen

Path C: SAP Cloud Identity Services

Layer 2: SAP Cloud Identity Services

Booster execution fails / formation errors

Path B: Joule Booster / Activation

Layer 4: Joule Service

Entitlements missing / systems not in landscape

Path A: SAP BTP Provisioning

Layer 7: SAP Business Technology Platform

Content Provider sync fails / unauthorized

Path D : Content Exposure & Roles

Layer 3: SAP Build Work Zone

The diagnostic path detailed will be mentioned in below chapter in part3.

Let us continue to part 3!

entry.title: Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
entry.content:
This blog is part3. The below series of the blogs are core RIG content on Joule Administration and we plan on keeping them up to date (such as with relevant information for SAP Managed Joule implementations in future),  which includes:

  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4

Phase 2: Diagnostic Investigation (Joule Administrator)

Based on the symptom category identified in Phase 1, follow the appropriate diagnostic path below.

Step 4a: Apply fixes if you would like self-resolvable.

  • Follow the corresponding Path (A-F) below in sequence, please check the details steps for each path;
  • Each step builds on the previous — do not skip steps

Step 5: Verify Fixes

  • If resolved, verify with a transactional prompt (e.g. "Display Sales Order 911");
  • Run health checks;
  • If not resolved after all steps, open SAP Support ticket with Correlation ID and traces together with step 4b to collect diagnostics

Step 4b: Collect Diagnostics with below necessary information

  • Correlation ID from traces
  • SAP Cloud Connector logs
  • SAP BTP Destination test
  • IPS job status

Step 6: Open SAP Support ticket with below necessary information

  • Use correct component
  • Attach traces & correlation ID
  • Include system version & steps

Diagnostic Paths

Path A: SAP BTP Provisioning Issues

This path resolves: missing Joule entitlements; missing systems in the visible landscape; and subaccount creation issues.

Step

Action

What to Check

A1

Verify Joule entitlements

SAP BTP Global Account > Entitlements > Service Assignments > Search "Joule"

A2

Verify system registration

SAP BTP Global Account > System Landscape > Systems

A3

Check data center compatibility

Data Centers Supported by Joule

A4

Validate subaccount configuration

Ensure Cloud Foundry (CF) enabled, correct region selected

Key SAP Notes:

  • 3639572 - Enabling Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
  • 3456718 - Menu "System Landscape" not visible 
  • 3661817 - SAP systems missing in System Landscape 

Path B: Joule Booster / Activation Issues

This path resolves: booster execution failures; missing formation prerequisites; and subscription errors.

Step

Action

What to Check

B1

Verify IAS tenant visibility

SAP BTP Global Account > System Landscape > Systems (IAS should be listed)

B2

Check IAS-Global Account linkage

Ensure IAS tenant matches SAP BTP Global Account's customer ID

B3

Verify SAP Build Work Zone subscription

Must be a subscription (not just an instance) in the Joule subaccount

B4

Trigger system sync

Click "Refresh" button in System Landscape if systems are missing

B5

Check formation type

Ensure correct capabilities selected in booster Step 4

Common errors and fixes:

SAP BTP Error Message

Fix

"Your global account does not have an Identity Authentication service tenant"

Configure trust between IAS and Joule subaccount.

SAP Note 3675105 - Joule Setup Error: "Your global account does not have an identity authentication service tenant"

"Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host..."

Ensure IAS linked to correct SAP BTP Global Account. Wait up to 1 week for sync.

SAP Note 3620409 - Could not find SAP Cloud Identity system with host: Joule booster error

"SUBSCRIBE_FAILED" for das-application

Unsubscribe and retry. If persistent, raise incident CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB.

SAP Note 3689452 - Entity Saas Subscription failed after running Joule Booster

Work Zone not in dropdown (Step 5)

Add SAP Build Work Zone subscription (not instance).

SAP Note 3710864 SAP Build Work Zone system not visible in dropdown during setup

SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Option not in Step 3 dropdown

Select in Step 4 "Select Capabilities" instead. SAP Note 3710921 - S/4HANA Private Cloud option is not listed in Step 3 - "Select Integration" in Joule Booster

Path C: SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS/IPS) Issues

This path resolves: single sign-on (SSO) issues; blank/empty login screens; mismatched user identities; and IPS synchronization failures.

Step

Action

What to Check

C1

Verify IAS trust domain

SAP BTP Joule Subaccount > Trust Configuration > Domain field must match SAP product domain

C2

Check IAS is used as proxy

Fiori Launchpad in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition must authenticate via IAS (not directly to corporate IDP)

C3

Validate Global User ID

Transaction SU01 > User > Goto > External User ID must match IAS Identity Directory Global User ID

C4

Check domain consistency

All systems must use same top-level domain (*.cloud.sap or *.ondemand.com)

C5

Verify IPS jobs

SAP Cloud Identity Services > Identity Provisioning > check job status and errors

C6

Validate transformations

Ensure IPS source/target transformations use latest version from SAP Help

Key indicators:

  • Joule not greeting with user's name → Global User ID mapping issue (Step C3)
  • Blank/white screen or IAS login prompt → Domain mismatch (Step C1). See  SAP Note 3635949 Blank white / IAS login screen appears when clicking on Joule icon from the solutions where Joule was integrated into

  •  

    "Corp IDP refused to connect" → IAS not configured as proxy for corporate Identity Provider (Step C2)

Path D：Content Exposure & Role Issues

This path resolves: wrong Joule response type (informational/direct instead of transactional/navigational); and missing Joule capabilities.

This is the most common category of issues encountered in production. Follow these steps sequentially:

Step

Action

What to Check

D1

Verify capability availability

Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition capabilities

D2

Check system version support

SAP 3523238 - Navigational and Transactional capabilities with Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition attachment, column F (minimum S/4HANA version per capability)

D3

Identify required Semantic Objects & Actions

SAP Note 3523238 attachment spreadsheet

D4

Verify business role contains required catalogs

Transaction PFCG > Role > Menu tab > Check business catalogs contain required Fiori App IDs

D5

Check content exposure

Transaction /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE > Verify role is exposed (V1 or V2)

D6

Verify CDM content readable

Test CDM URL directly: /sap/bc/http/ui2/flp_content_exposure/entities (V2) or /sap/bc/ui2/cdm3/entities (V1)

D7

Refresh Channel Manager

SAP Build Work Zone Admin > Channel Manager > Update/Refresh

D8

Verify apps in Content Manager

SAP Build Work Zone Admin > Content Manager > Find role > Check Semantic Objects & Actions

D9

Check user role assignment in SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build Work Zone Admin > Settings > Identity Provisioning tab > Search user > Verify roles listed

D10

Verify OData services activated

Check APIs listed in SAP Note 3523238 are active (transaction SICF or /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE)

Important notes:

  • When using custom business roles, make sure they mimic the content of SAP business roles. They must reference the same Launchpad Application Descriptor Items (LADIs) (aka tile/target mapping definitions) as the original SAP business role.For Master/Derived Role models, expose the Master Role only (derived roles cannot be exposed independently).
  • After a system upgrade, you must refresh Joule's capability catalog by excluding and re-including the system in the Joule formation.
  • Schedule a regular content update job to keep CDM content synchronized.
  • See what to check in these SAP Learning Videos: "Resolving Missing Business Roles for Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition" and "Resolving Inactive ICF and OData Services for Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition".

Path E: Connectivity & Principal Propagation Issues

This path resolves: "I'm having trouble connecting" errors; "backend not reachable" errors; and timeout errors.

Step

Action

What to Check

E1

Test Principal Propagation

Copy S4HANA_PCE_SSO destination as "authtest", add HTML5.DynamicDestination=true, test CDM URL through SAP Build Work Zone

E2

Check SAP Cloud Connector

Verify system mapping, certificate validity, principal type = "X.509 certificate"

E3

Enable SAP Cloud Connector logs

Turn on tunnel trace and TLS trace for detailed error information

E4

Check SAP BTP destinations

Verify S4HANA_PCE_SSO destination configuration and connection test

E5

Verify trusted domains

Add SAP Fiori Launchpad URL to IAS Trusted Domains AND SAP BTP Security > Settings

E6

Trace in backend

Transaction /n/IWFND/TRACES for HTTP payload trace; SMICM for HTTP trace level 1

E7

Check CERTRULE

Transaction CERTRULE for rule-based certificate mappings

Troubleshooting resources:

  • How to troubleshoot SAP Cloud Connector Principal Propagation over HTTPS
  • Pro tip: Enable SAP Cloud Connector Alerting in production to receive email notifications 30 days before certificates expire. This prevents connectivity outages proactively.

Path F: UX / Icon Appearance Issues

This path resolves: Joule icon not visible; Joule icon displayed incorrectly; and Joule icon is visible but does not launch the Joule dialog.

Step

Action

What to Check

F1

Check 3rd party cookies

Ensure browser allows 3rd party cookies for the Joule/IAS domains

F2

Verify SAPUI5 version

Must be equal to or higher than minimum supported version. 

See SAP Note 3641945 - Joule icon displayed wrongly in Fiori launchpad of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

F3

Check user role for Joule plug-in

Ensure user has a single role containing the SAP Fiori business catalogue for Joule

F4

Clear caches

Browser cache + server cache via program /UI2/INVALIDATE_GLOBAL_CACHES

F5

Check SAP Fiori customizing conflicts

Transaction /UI2/FLC - check "Configuration" first, then "Customizing"

F6

Verify trusted domains

Add URLs to IAS Tenant Settings > Trusted Domains AND SAP BTP subaccount > Security > Settings

Continued with part4 on the issue summary and key takeways.

entry.title: Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4
entry.content:
this blog is part4. The below series of the blogs are core RIG content on Joule Administration and we plan on keeping them up to date (such as with relevant information for SAP Managed Joule implementations in future),  which includes:

  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part1
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part2
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part3
  • Troubleshooting SAP Joule: A Practical Guide for Administrators - part4

Issue Catalog Summary

In this github, you’ll find a consolidated catalog of documented Joule issues, organized by architecture layer to make navigation as intuitive as possible. For the full picture — including detailed root causes, resolutions, SAP Notes, support components - just git and download the attached Excel file. And we’d love to keep this resource growing: if you have additional issues to contribute, you’re warmly invited to add the valid ones.

[Github] leee03/SAP_Joule_Issue_Catalog

Layer 1: Client / UX

  • Frame-ancestors Content Security Policy (CSP) violation
  • Joule icon not showing in SAP Fiori Launchpad
  • Icon displayed incorrectly (SAPUI5 version)
  • Unable to launch Joule via icon (cookies)
  • Icon not appearing for specific user (SAP SuccessFactors)
  • Blank/greyed-out screen (SAP SuccessFactors)

Layer 2: SAP Cloud Identity Services

  • Corporate Identity Provider refused to connect
  • Joule not greeting with user's name
  • Blank/white IAS login screen
  • IPS transformation editing fails
  • IAS source system read job failing

Layer 3: SAP Build Work Zone

  • Content Manager not visible (foundation plan limitation)
  • Content provider sync fails (unauthorized)
  • Navigation links not working (proxy type)
  • Capabilities limited with foundation vs standard plan
  • Joule cannot return transactional/navigational responses
  • SuccessFactors navigational use cases not working - profile button missing, deep links not generated

Layer 4: Joule Service

  • IAS tenant not found during booster prerequisites
  • Cloud Identity system host not found
  • IAS system missing from System Landscape
  • Build Work Zone not in booster dropdown
  • SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Option missing from booster
  • SaaS subscription failed (das-application)
  • Formation creation error (IAS not enabled in Work Zone)
  • Joule scenario selected but execution fails with empty response/error/timeout

Layer 5: Connectivity

  • "I'm having trouble connecting" error
  • SAP Cloud Connector cannot connect to subaccount
  • "Digital Assistant not found" error
  • Runtime destination proxy type incorrectly set causing navigation link failures

Layer 6: Backend System

  • Required OData services not activated
  • Business roles missing required semantic objects
  • Content exposure not configured correctly
  • Custom roles not working with Joule - missing semantic objects, App IDs, or wrong CDM exposure

Layer 7: SAP Business Technology Platform

  • Joule service not available in subaccount entitlement
  • Entitlements not available despite supported data center
  • System Landscape menu not visible
  • SAP systems not visible in SAP BTP System Landscape
  • E2E setup requires high manual effort due to documentation gaps

SAP Support Components Reference

When self-resolution is not possible, raise an SAP incident using the appropriate component:

Component

Scope

CA-JOULE

General Joule service issues and functionality

CA-JOULE-PRV-ONB

Joule Subscription, Booster, Provisioning errors

CA-JOULE-PRV / CA-JOULE-PRV-INT

Provisioning and integrations with applications

CA-FLP-EXT-PCE-JOULE

Joule integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

CA-JOULE-CLT

Joule Messaging Client related topics

CA-JOULE-CLT-COP

Joule x Microsoft Copilot integration

CA-JOULE-CR

Joule Conversational Runtime

CA-JOULE-CI

Joule Conversation Logging and Insights

CA-JOULE-TA

Tenant Administration / Content Deployment

CA-JOULE-TR

Tenant Runtime (Bot-runtime), Approuter issues

BC-CP-MP-FOR

Unified Customer Landscape (UCL) / Formations

BC-IAM-IDS

SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication (IAS)

BC-IAM-IPS

SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning (IPS)

EP-WZ-ONB / EP-WZ-STA

SAP Build Work Zone

LOD-SF-PLT-JOU

Joule in SAP SuccessFactors

 BC-MID-SCC

SAP Cloud Connector

When raising a ticket, always include:

  • SAP S/4HANA system name and release version
  • Correlation ID (from Joule traces)
  • Downloaded trace .zip file
  • Screenshots of the error
  • Steps to reproduce

See SAP Note 3666014 - Information required for Joule troubleshooting for the complete list of information SAP Support requires.

Troubleshooting Toolkit: Verify Your Configuration

Here is the troubleshooting toolkit organized by architecture layer:

Layer 1: Client / UX

  • Joule Download Traces (Settings > About)
  • Correlation ID (last 5-10 messages in trace)
  • Transaction /UI2/FLC — SAP Fiori Launchpad content check
  • Transaction /UI2/INVALIDATE_GLOBAL_CACHES — Cache clear

Layer 2: SAP Cloud Identity Services

  • IAS Admin Console (authentication logs, trust configuration)
  • IPS Admin Console (provisioning job status, transformation errors)

Layer 3: SAP Build Work Zone

  • SAP Build Work Zone Navigation API

Layer 4: Joule Service

  • Joule Booster logs (execution history, error details)
  • Formation configuration status in SAP BTP Cockpit

Layer 5: Connectivity

  • Transaction SMICM — ICM trace (Principal Propagation)
  • SAP Cloud Connector Logs (tunnel/TLS trace)
  • SAP BTP Destination — Connection Test

Layer 6: Backend System

  • Transaction /n/IWFND/TRACES — HTTP Payload trace
  • Transaction /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE — Content Exposure

Layer 7: SAP Business Technology Platform

  • SAP BTP Cockpit UI (entitlements, system landscape, subaccount config)

Health Checks: Validate Your Configuration

Before onboarding business users, perform these quick validation tests or dobule checks after apply fixes:

  • Test a transactional capability: Prompt Joule with "Display Sales Order 911" (or a valid order number). If Joule returns actual business data, the end-to-end configuration is working.
  • Test Principal Propagation: Use the CDM URL test through SAP Build Work Zone as described in Path E above.
  • Test content exposure: Access the CDM URL directly in a browser with the technical user (V1: http://<server>:<port>/sap/bc/ui2/cdm3/entities or V2: http://<server>:<port>/sap/bc/http/ui2/flp_content_exposure/entities)
  • Verify user roles in SAP Build Work Zone: SAP Build Work Zone Admin > Settings > Identity Provisioning > Search for user > Confirm roles are listed.
  • Check Navigation API: Access the navigation API to verify intent-based navigation targets.
  • All the details in SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Joule health checks

Key Takeaways

Start Here: Identify the Problem Layer

Before fixing anything, figure out where the issue lives. This saves time and prevents fixing the wrong thing.

Most Common Problem

Joule gives information instead of completing tasks =  This is almost always a role/content exposure/IPS sync issue (Path D).

Quick Checklist (Check These First)

  •  Are user roles synced to SAP Build Work Zone?
  •  Is the content accessible/exposed?
  •  Is Principal Propagation working?

Prevention

  • Turn on Cloud Connector alerts
  • Schedule regular content sync jobs
  • Refresh systems after updates

Before Contacting SAP Support

  1. Download Joule traces
  2. Save the Correlation ID
  3. Send both with your ticket

In short: Troubleshoot systematically, check permissions first, monitor regularly, and collect error details before reaching out for support.

Additional Resources

  • SAP Help: Joule Portal
  • SAP Help: Joule Constraints
  • SAP Help: Monitoring and Troubleshooting
  • Reference Architecture: Identity and Access Management for Joule
  • 3679305 - Joule configuration checking when it cannot return the response of transactional or navigational capability prompts
  • Joule Roadmap

Acknowledgments

This blog consolidates insights from the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and Regional Implementation Group, the AI RIG team, and the Joule product teams. Special thanks to colleagues and the broader SAP community who continue to share their experiences.

We will update this blog as new issues and resolutions are identified. If you have questions or feedback, please leave a comment below.

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Customer Care and RIG team

entry.title: Enabling SAP Joule on an Existing SAP Build Work Zone Standard Edition Site
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog documents the actual steps we performed to enable SAP Joule on an already-running SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition site.
This was not a greenfield setup. SAP Build Work Zone was already live, the runtime site was already being used, and the landscape already had SAP Cloud Identity Services / IAS configuration in place. The goal was to enable Joule using the Joule foundation plan without creating a new SAP Build Work Zone site or disrupting the existing Work Zone access model.
The final result was successful: the existing SAP Build Work Zone runtime site showed the Joule icon, and the Joule side panel opened successfully.

Starting Point

We started with an existing SAP Build Work Zone setup. The key point in our approach was to reuse the existing SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition instance rather than create a new Work Zone site.
During the architecture discussion, SAP confirmed that we did not need to create a new SAP Build Work Zone instance and that the existing SAP Build Work Zone standard edition setup could be reused for Joule integration.

Technical Prerequisites and Data Center Checks

Before we started the Joule booster activity, we validated the technical placement of SAP Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, the SAP BTP subaccount, and SAP Cloud Identity Services / IAS.
This was important because our setup was not a new Work Zone deployment. We already had multiple SAP Build Work Zone standard edition sites running across different BTP subaccounts, and the goal was to enable Joule on the existing Work Zone site instead of creating a new runtime entry point.

1. SAP Build Work Zone and Joule Must Be Aligned to the Correct Subaccount
During the architecture clarification, SAP confirmed that we did not need to create a new SAP Build Work Zone site. We could reuse the existing SAP Build Work Zone standard edition instance, provided Joule was enabled in the same relevant BTP setup used by that Work Zone instance.

For our pilot, the target environment was:

BTP Subaccount: btp-qa
SAP Build Work Zone: Existing standard edition site
Backend: S/4HANA QA / QA1 client 100 site
Joule Plan: Foundation

The Joule foundation plan was added to the btp-qa subaccount before running the Joule booster.

2. Validate Joule-Supported Data Center
We also checked whether the selected BTP region was supported for Joule.
In our project discussion, SAP confirmed that Joule could be used from supported regions such as US21 Azure or US10 AWS, and that the Joule/BTP subaccount region must be a supported Joule data center.

US10 - US East (VA), Amazon Web Services
US21 - US East (VA), Microsoft Azure
US30 - US Central (IA), Google Cloud Platform
EU10 - Europe (Frankfurt), Amazon Web Services
EU20 - Europe (Netherlands), Microsoft Azure
EU30 - Europe (Frankfurt), Google Cloud Platform
AP10 - Australia (Sydney), Amazon Web Services
AP20 - Australia (Sydney), Microsoft Azure
AP30 - Australia (Sydney), Google Cloud Platform
JP10 - Japan (Tokyo), Amazon Web Services
AP11 - Singapore, Amazon Web Services
AP21 - Singapore, Microsoft Azure
BR10 - Brazil (São Paulo), Amazon Web Services
IN30 - India (Mumbai), Google Cloud Platform

SAP Help should always be checked for the latest supported list before starting a new setup, because supported regions may change.
For our pilot, the Work Zone runtime subaccounts were in US21 / Microsoft Azure, which is confirmed as a Joule-supported region as per SAP documentation.

3. Confirm the Actual Work Zone Runtime Subaccount
One key clarification we made was to distinguish between the subaccounts that actually host the Work Zone runtime sites and the supporting Cloud Identity Services-related subaccounts.
Our active SAP Build Work Zone standard edition runtime subaccounts were documented as follows:

DEV → btp-dev → us21 / Microsoft Azure
QA → btp-qa → us21 / Microsoft Azure
PREPROD → btp-preprod → us21 / Microsoft Azure
PROD → btp-prod → us21 / Microsoft Azure

Each of these Work Zone subaccounts was connected to its corresponding S/4HANA backend landscape. We also had separate Cloud Identity Services support subaccounts in us20 / US West WA, but those were not the primary SAP Build Work Zone runtime subaccounts used by business users. This distinction mattered because enabling Joule in a new or wrong subaccount would not automatically enable Joule in the existing Work Zone runtime site that users already access.

4. Do Not Create a Separate Work Zone Site Just for Joule
We explicitly reviewed whether we should create a new Joule subaccount with a new SAP Build Work Zone subscription.
That approach was rejected for our use case because it would create a separate Work Zone site, and the existing DEV, QA, PREPROD, and PROD Work Zone sites would not automatically inherit Joule. This preserved the existing user access model and avoided creating a fifth Work Zone site.


5. Validate IAS Tenant and Domain Consistency
We confirmed that the same SAP Cloud Identity Services / IAS tenant and domain should be used consistently across SAP Build Work Zone, Joule, and the integrated SAP solutions.
In our case, the IAS tenant was hosted in USA, Quincy, WA. SAP confirmed that the IAS tenant could still be used with Joule running in supported BTP regions such as US21 or US10, as long as the same IAS tenant/domain was used consistently across the integrated systems.

6. Confirm OIDC Trust Before Running Joule Setup
Another technical prerequisite was the trust protocol.
Our existing QA trust configuration used SAML for the custom IAS identity provider. Before proceeding, we asked SAP whether SAML was sufficient for Joule with SAP Build Work Zone standard edition. SAP confirmed that OpenID Connect trust between the BTP subaccount and SAP Cloud Identity Services / IAS was mandatory.
Because of this, the QA environment had to be migrated from SAML to OIDC before we could complete the Joule setup.
The prerequisite we applied was:

BTP Subaccount Trust

Existing: SAML

Required for Joule: OpenID Connect

7. Validate One Work Zone Instance per Joule Setup
We also clarified the deployment model for multiple Work Zone environments. Our landscape had separate Work Zone sites for DEV, QA, PREPROD, and PROD. SAP confirmed that the existing Work Zone standard edition setup could be reused, but the Joule setup needed to be aligned to the relevant Work Zone subaccount/site rather than expecting one Joule setup to automatically light up all Work Zone subaccounts.

btp-qa
├── Existing SAP Build Work Zone standard edition
├── Joule foundation plan
└── QA1 / client 100 runtime validation

Step 1: Confirm Joule Entitlement

The first technical activity was to validate that the Joule entitlement was available.
Validate Joule entitlement
Add the Joule service plan to the btp-qa subaccount
Use the foundation plan for Joule

This step was important because the Joule booster could not be run successfully until the entitlement and service plan were available in the target BTP subaccount.

Step 2: Add Joule Foundation Plan to the Target Subaccount

After validating entitlement, we added the Joule service plan to the btp-qa subaccount.

The plan selected during setup was:

Joule Plan: Foundation

Step 3: Run the Joule Booster

After the Joule foundation plan was available, we ran the Joule booster from the SAP BTP global account.
The steps performed were:

Log in to the SAP BTP account as global account administrator.
Open the SAP BTP global account.
In the left navigation, select Boosters.
Search for Setting up Joule or Setting Up Joule - Preview.
Start the booster.
Complete the booster flow: Check Prerequisites->Set up Subaccount->Select Integrations->Select Capabilities

During the booster flow, the relevant subaccount was selected for Joule provisioning and configuration. The Joule plan shown in the setup was Foundation.

Step 4: Address the IAS Trust Requirement

One of the major items identified during the project was the trust protocol requirement.
The existing btp-qa trust configuration was using SAML for the custom IAS identity provider. We asked SAP whether SAML was sufficient or whether OpenID Connect was required. SAP confirmed that OpenID Connect trust between the BTP subaccount and SAP Cloud Identity Services / IAS was mandatory for Joule integration with SAP Build Work Zone standard edition.
SAP also confirmed that the OIDC trust should be established using the same IAS tenant/domain already used for SAP Build Work Zone.

Step 5: Migrate QA Trust from SAML to OIDC

After confirming the requirement, the btp-qa BTP subaccount trust configuration was migrated to the OIDC protocol. Once this was completed, we proceeded with the Joule booster setup. However, after the booster execution, the formation showed a synchronization error.
The error indicated that synchronization between SAP Build Work Zone and SAP Cloud Identity Services had failed because an IAS application could not be found.

Step 6: Investigate Formation Synchronization Error

After migrating QA to OIDC and running the Joule booster, the btp-qa formation produced a synchronization error.
The error referenced:
SAP Build Work Zone system
SAP Cloud Identity Services system
Tenant mapping notification failure
IAS application not found
Formation ID provided in the error message

At this stage, we reviewed the related SAP Note/KBA referenced : SAP Note 3539985. This note indicated a possible issue with older SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition subscriptions created before March 20, 2025.

The resolution was to enable Identity Authentication inside SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition:
SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition
→ Settings
→ Identity Authentication
→ Enable

Step 7: Enable SAP Build Work Zone Identity Authentication Setting

Based on the investigation, we enabled the SAP Build Work Zone Identity Authentication setting for the older SAP Build Work Zone subscription.

The final update confirmed that after enabling this setting, we revalidated the Joule formation and completed the remaining configurations.
This was one of the key fixes in the pilot.
The setting enabled was: SAP Build Work Zone Identity Authentication setting = Enabled

Step 8: Resolve Duplicate IAS Trust Configuration Issue

Another issue occurred during formation setup.

Cannot update IAS trust configuration for subdomain btp-qa
status: 409
message: IDP with name 'saml2iasqa' exists.

To resolve this, the inactive trust entry was renamed to include “old”, and the formation was rerun. After that, the formation completed successfully.
This was an important cleanup step because the previous inactive trust configuration was still causing a conflict during the Joule/IAS setup.

Step 9: Add Required Trusted Domains

As part of the remaining setup, required trusted domains were added in the BTP subaccount security settings. Please noet that we need to add Joule instance url as well. <<You can get this under formation page>>

 

Step 10: Validate Joule Formation Status

After enabling the Work Zone Identity Authentication setting, resolving the trust conflict, adding trusted domains, and completing the remaining setup, the Joule formation was validated again.

The formation included:

SAP Cloud Identity Services
SAP Build Work Zone
Joule
SAP SuccessFactors

Although this blog focuses on SAP Build Work Zone, the actual formation created during the pilot also included SAP SuccessFactors

Step 11: Enable Joule in SAP Build Work Zone Site Settings

After the formation was ready, Joule was enabled in the existing SAP Build Work Zone site settings.
Services → Joule = Yes

The performed configuration was therefore:
SAP Build Work Zone Site Settings
→ Identity Authentication setting = Enabled
→ Services
→ Joule = Yes

Step 12: Validate the Runtime Site

After the Work Zone site settings were updated, the runtime site was opened and validated.
The final status confirmed:

The existing SAP Build Work Zone runtime site showed the Joule icon.
The Joule side panel opened successfully.

This confirmed that the Joule activation on the existing SAP Build Work Zone site was successful.

Issues Faced and How We Resolved Them

Issue 1: Existing Trust Was SAML-Based
What Happened
The existing btp-qa trust configuration used SAML for the custom IAS identity provider. We needed confirmation whether this was acceptable for Joule.
Resolution
SAP confirmed that OpenID Connect trust was mandatory for Joule integration with SAP Build Work Zone standard edition. The QA environment was then migrated from SAML to OIDC before proceeding.

Issue 2: Formation Synchronization Failed After OIDC Migration
What Happened
After the OIDC migration and Joule booster setup, the formation synchronization failed between SAP Build Work Zone and SAP Cloud Identity Services.
The error mentioned that an IAS application was not found during assignment processing.
Resolution
We reviewed the referenced SAP Note/KBA 3539985 and identified that the SAP Build Work Zone Identity Authentication setting was disabled in BWZ site setting. This was relevant because the Work Zone subscription was older. We enabled the Identity Authentication setting in SAP Build Work Zone, then revalidated the Joule formation.

Issue 3: Duplicate IAS Trust Entry Blocked Formation
What Happened
We encountered formation failure caused by an IAS trust configuration conflict:
Plain TextIDP with name 'saml2iasqa' exists. The error occurred while updating IAS trust configuration for the btp-qa subdomain. This was because we had old SAML based protocal same name as new IODC protocol. 
Resolution
The inactive duplicate trust entry was renamed to include “old”. After that, the formation was rerun and completed successfully.

Issue 4: Required Trusted Domains Were Missing
What Happened
Trusted domains had to be added as part of the remaining BTP security configuration.
Resolution
The required trusted domains were added in the BTP subaccount security settings. 

Lessons Learned

1. Existing Work Zone Can Be Reused - We did not create a new SAP Build Work Zone site. The existing SAP Build Work Zone standard edition site was reused for Joule enablement. SAP confirmed that this was acceptable for our scenario.
2. OIDC Was Required - The existing SAML trust was not sufficient for our Joule setup. We had to move the QA trust configuration to OIDC before completing the setup.
3. Older Work Zone Subscriptions May Need the Identity Authentication Setting Enabled - In BTP, the SAP Build Work Zone Identity Authentication setting was disabled. After enabling it, the Joule formation could be revalidated and completed.
4. Inactive Trust Entries Can Still Cause Problems - Even inactive or old trust entries can cause conflicts during formation setup. In our case, an inactive trust entry with the same IDP name caused a 409 conflict. Renaming the inactive entry resolved the issue.
5. Formation Ready Status Was a Key Milestone - The setup was not considered complete until the Joule formation reached Ready status.

Final Outcome

After completing the above steps, the btp-qa SAP Build Work Zone runtime site, specifically the QA1 client 200 site, successfully displayed the Joule icon.
The Joule side panel opened successfully from the existing SAP Build Work Zone runtime site. The Joule formation was in Ready status, and the required Work Zone site settings were enabled.
This confirmed that Joule was successfully enabled on the existing SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition site without creating a new Work Zone runtime site.

entry.title: Inconsistent Behavior of SAP Build Work Zone Fiori Apps on Mobile (Joule Work App & Browser Access)?
entry.content:
Introduction

In our recent implementation of the My Time Sheet application via SAP Build Work Zone, we conducted multiple testing sessions after enabling external (internet) access.

While the application performs well in controlled environments, we observed inconsistent and non-deterministic behavior across mobile devices, browsers, and the Joule Work app. In some cases, the application works seamlessly, while in others, it results in partial loading or blank screens.

This article summarizes our findings and highlights key architectural considerations for the SAP community.

Architecture Overview

To provide full context, the solution is implemented using a standard SAP architecture with the following components:

  • Authentication: Azure Active Directory (Azure AD)
  • Authorization & Trust: SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
  • Frontend Runtime: SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition)
  • Mobile Access: Joule Work App
  • Backend Connectivity: SAP Web Dispatcher with Principal Propagation
  • Application Type: HTML5 / SAPUI5 (Fiori-based)

End-to-End Flow (Simplified)

Mobile / Browser
      ↓
Azure AD (Authentication)
      ↓
IAS (Trust & Authorization)
      ↓
SAP Build Work Zone (HTML5 App)
      ↓
Destination Service
      ↓
Web Dispatcher (Principal Propagation)
      ↓
Backend (e.g., S/4HANA)

This architecture introduces multiple authentication hops, session dependencies, and runtime layers, which may contribute to instability in mobile scenarios.

Testing Scope

Testing was performed across:

  • iOS devices (work & personal)
  • Android devices (work & personal profiles)
  • Work and private laptops (Chrome & Edge)
  • Access methods:
    • Direct URL (browser)
    • Joule Work mobile application

Observed Behavior

1. iOS (iPhone)

Browser Access

  • ✅ Works in Microsoft Edge
  • ✅ Works in Google app
  • ❌ Not working in Chrome

Joule Work App

  • ❌ App opens but remains blank / non-functional

2. Android

Browser Access

  • ✅ Works consistently

Joule Work App

  • ✅ Initially works
  • ⚠️ Stops working after some time (session inconsistency)

3. Desktop (Laptops)

  • ✅ Works reliably in Chrome
  • ⚠️ In Microsoft Edge, tiles sometimes fail to load initially but work after refresh

Key Issues Identified

1. Inconsistent Behavior

  • Same application behaves differently:
    • Across devices (iOS vs Android)
    • Across browsers (Edge vs Chrome)
    • Across sessions (intermittent failures)

2. Blank Screen Issue

  • Observed in both:
    • Browser (after successful launch)
    • Joule Work app (application shell loads, but content is empty)

3. Strong Browser Dependency

  • Chrome on iOS consistently fails
  • Edge and Google app perform better

4. Mobile App Instability (Joule Work)

  • iOS: Not working
  • Android: Works intermittently

Architectural Observations

Based on the current setup, the issue appears to be architectural rather than purely configuration-related.

1. Multi-Layer Authentication Complexity

  • Authentication via Azure AD
  • Trust and session handling via IAS
  • Backend access via Principal Propagation (Web Dispatcher)

This creates:

  • Multiple token exchanges
  • Session dependencies across layers
  • Increased chances of session misalignment

2. Session Handling in Mobile Environments

  • SAP Build Work Zone relies on:
    • Browser-based sessions
    • Cookies and storage

Mobile platforms (especially iOS):

  • Restrict cookies (SameSite policies)
  • Isolate sessions in WebView containers

Result:

  • Session loss
  • Token expiration not handled gracefully
  • Blank screen scenarios

3. Joule Work App Limitations

  • Likely uses a WebView container
  • WebView environments:
    • Do not fully share authentication context with browser sessions

Particularly problematic in:

  • Multi-hop authentication (Azure AD → IAS → Work Zone)

4. Distributed Runtime Dependencies

Application flow includes:

  • Work Zone
  • HTML5 App Repository
  • Destination Service
  • Web Dispatcher
  • Backend system

👉 Any intermittent failure in this chain:

  • Results in incomplete UI rendering
  • No clear error feedback to user

5. iOS Platform Constraints

  • All browsers use WebKit
  • Strict enforcement of:
    • Cookie handling
    • Session persistence

 Leading to:

  • Chrome not working
  • Inconsistent app behavior

Conclusion

While the application performs well under stable conditions, the current setup shows:

  • ❌ Inconsistent behavior across devices and browsers
  • ❌ Limited reliability for mobile usage
  • ⚠️ High dependency on session handling across multiple layers

This makes the solution not fully stable for productive mobile usage at this stage.

Questions to the SAP Community

I would appreciate insights on the following:

  1. Is this behavior a known limitation when using:

    • Azure AD + IAS
    • Work Zone + Joule Work app
    • Principal Propagation via Web Dispatcher

  2. Is Joule Work app officially recommended for:

    • Work Zone HTML5/Fiori apps in such architectures?

  3. What is the recommended mobile strategy?

    • SAP Mobile Start?
    • SAP MDK-based applications?

  1. Are there best practices for:

    • Stabilizing session handling across Azure AD and IAS?
    • Avoiding blank screen issues in Work Zone apps?

  2. Has anyone implemented a similar architecture successfully with consistent mobile behavior?

Final Thought

This experience highlights an important architectural insight:

SAP Build Work Zone works reliably in browser-based environments, but mobile usage—especially with external authentication providers and multi-hop architectures—requires careful alignment with SAP’s recommended mobile strategy.

entry.title: Creating an SAP SD Billing Document Using a BAPI in SAP Build and Accessing It Through 'Joule Work'
entry.content:
Hi Everyone!

Recently, I was working on a requirement to create an SD billing document in SAP S/4HANA using SAP Build Process Automation and make it accessible through the Joule Work mobile app.

Due to some internal requirements & technical restrictions, the only option available to me was to use the BAPI_BILLINGDOC_CREATEMULTIPLE BAPI to create the billing document.

I am sharing the following demo which I created in the test environment. To use this BAPI in SAP Build, there are two main configurations required:

1. Map the virtual host to the internal host in SAP Cloud Connector using the RFC protocol, and expose the required BAPI or RFC function module.

2. Create a destination of type RFC in the SAP BTP Cockpit.

Let's first see how Cloud to On-Premises mapping in Cloud Connector looks like:

We also need to enable the required resources, such as the BAPIs and function modules that we want to consume in SAP Build. The RFC_FUNCTION_SEARCH function module enables the search for function modules from SAP Build.



And this is how the destination in BTP looks:



The virtual message server is configured in the Cloud Connector and mapped to the internal SAP S/4HANA host, and logon group information is available in SMLG.



Logon group can be found here :



Now that the Cloud Connector and BTP destination configurations are complete, let’s log in to SAP Build and see how we can create an action using BAPI_BILLINGDOC_CREATEMULTIPLE.

Select RFC's, BAPI's:



All the BAPIs & RFCs from backend will be available to use. We will go ahead and look for BAPI/RFC we'd like to use, and create a new project.



The action is now available in Connectors -> Actions, and can be used in our build projects.



Using this action, I created a custom process that can be triggered to generate a billing document by entering the reference sales document number.



Let's see it in action;
Assume we have below sales order, for which we'd like to generate a billing document.



Let’s now access the custom process we created. I have added the form URL to SAP Build Work Zone for easy access.





Since I added an email notification step to the process, we receive an email once the billing document is generated successfully.



Let's validated the generated billing document number in the system.



To access SAP Build Work Zone applications and tiles in the Joule mobile app, open Settings, scan the QR code, and complete the authentication process.





As we can see below, all our SAP Build Work Zone applications are now accessible through the Joule Work mobile app as well.





Thanks for reading!

Stay tuned for more demos on AI agents using Joule Studio, n8n, and automation in SAP Build.

entry.title: SAP Build Work Zone as UI Integration Layer: A Navigation Paradigm Powered by Exposure V2
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Build Work Zone has long been the front door for the SAP landscape, providing a unified entry point to applications across multiple SAP backends systems. Following our learnings over the years in large-scale customer implementation, we have recently updated the UX integration layer paradigm. With this, SAP Build Work Zone sits on-top the individual product shells, rather than replacing them. As a result, end users benefit from having SAP Build Work Zone as the access point to the SAP landscape, while keep gaining the power of the lines of business specific home pages with their unique capabilities. 

Exposure version 2, available as of SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 and SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2402, is the technical vehicle that makes this new paradigm possible. In this blog post, I'll explore what this paradigm shift means for customers, how exposure version 2 enables it, what benefits it delivers, and what prerequisites and differences in behavior you should be aware of before adopt.

 

The Traditional Paradigm: One Shell to Run All Applications

In the traditional integration model — supported by exposure version 1 — SAP Build Work Zone acted as a replacement shell. Applications from SAP S/4HANA and other connected systems were launched in-place, meaning they opened embedded directly within the Work Zone shell. Navigation between apps, shell services, and all user interactions happened inside this single, centralized environment.

While this approach offered a unified look and feel, it came with significant trade-offs:

  • Loss of product-specific shell capabilities: Features native to the SAP S/4HANA shell — such as the very popular SAP S/4HANA My Home, built-in support ticket creation, Microsoft Teams integration, and advanced shell plugins — were unavailable when apps ran inside the Work Zone shell.
  • Navigation complexity: Users who needed to work intensively within a single backend system found themselves constrained by a shell that wasn't designed for deep, product-specific workflows.
  • Content integration challenges: Exposure v1 relies on a deprecated content model in S/4HANA which might lead to artificial app splits and inconsistencies with the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library.

 

The New Paradigm: Work Zone as a UI Integration Layer

Based on extensive user research, SAP discovered that experienced system users prefer to work within their individual product shells. These users don't want their SAP S/4HANA launchpad replaced — they want quick access to cross-system services and then the ability to dive into the product environment they know best.

This insight led to a repositioning of SAP Build Work Zone. Rather than being the single shell for everything, it now serves as a cross-product integration layer focused on providing a starting point that gives users the ability to discover and launch apps across multiple SAP systems and third-party solutions without needing to know which backend hosts them. It offers unified access to central services, e.g. a central search across connected systems so users can find content regardless of where it resides, access to SAP Task Center which delivers a consolidated task inbox that aggregates approvals and workflows from multiple backends, and centralized notifications.

Once a user selects an application, they are directed to the native product shell (e.g., the SAP S/4HANA Fiori launchpad) where the app opens in a new browser tab. All subsequent navigation — app-to-app transitions, shell services, and product-specific features — happens within that product's own environment. Users return to SAP Build Work Zone simply by closing the tab.

This approach creates a clear separation of concerns: Work Zone handles cross-system orchestration, while individual product shells handle product-specific workflows.

 

How Exposure v2 Enables This Paradigm

Exposure v2 was purpose-built to support this integration layer model. Here's how its design choices map directly to the new paradigm:

Design Choice in Exposure v2

How It Supports the Integration Layer Paradigm

Apps launch in the content provider's native shell (new browser tab)

Users land in the product-specific environment with full access to all shell capabilities — no feature loss.

Navigation managed by the content provider shell

App-to-app navigation within SAP S/4HANA stays within S/4HANA, creating cohesive navigation flow

Consistent app model aligned with SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library

Apps exposed to Work Zone match what users see in the reference library, eliminating artificial splits and confusion.

Semantic App IDs (transaction codes or Fiori IDs)

Apps are easily traceable and identifiable, simplifying administration and troubleshooting across the integration landscape.

Business are directly derived from the new content model

Reflects the modern SAP S/4HANA content architecture, reducing integration errors and maintenance overhead.

Meanwhile, for self-service applications targeting larger or more casual user groups — such as leave requests, time sheet management, or onboarding workflows — administrators can still manually integrate these apps to launch in-place within Work Zone. While the decision for exposure version 1 or 2 is taken on content provider level, this hybrid approach provides additional flexibility: power users get their native product experience, while occasional users get a simple, guided experience without needing to understand backend systems.

 

Benefits of the Integration Layer Approach

Full Access to Product-Specific Capabilities

With apps launching in their native shell, users benefit from all the that SAP S/4HANA (or other products) provide — including advanced shell plugins, built-in support for ticket creation, Microsoft Teams integration, SAP Analytics Cloud embedded apps, and advanced features like Variant Configuration (AVC) or Advanced Available-to-Promise (ATP) components that are not supported with in-place launching.

Clear Navigation Model

The new exposure model helps reduce navigation flaws by placing navigation responsibility squarely with SAP S/4HANA. Rather than layering competing navigation paradigms, the model leverages a pattern users already know: opening a product in a new browser tab and closing it to return. This is a well-established convention used by other vendors who organize navigation to secondary products in the same way, making the experience immediately familiar. Each product is clearly , so there is no ambiguity about which product is in control.

Reduced Maintenance Overhead

Because exposure v2 aligns with the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library and uses semantic IDs, administrators spend less time troubleshooting content mismatches, artificial app splits, or broken tile/target mapping combinations.

Flexibility for Diverse User Groups

The paradigm doesn't force an all-or-nothing approach. Exposure v2 only supports the launch in the native product shell, but customers can still integrate cross-system services and casual-user apps manually to have them run in-place, while business-critical transactional apps launch in their native shells. This lets organizations tailor the experience to different user personas.

 

Key Considerations and Constraints

While the integration layer paradigm and exposure v2 offer compelling advantages, there are important constraints customers must evaluate before adoption. First, exposure v2 is only available for SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 or later (on-premise) and SAP S/4HANA public Cloud. Older SAP S/4HANA releases, SAP ECC, and SAP Business Suite systems do not support v2. For these systems, the traditional in-place model with exposure v1 remains the only option.

SAP Fiori Launchpad Content Based on Launchpad App Description

Exposure v2 relies on the Launchpad App Descriptor Item (LADI) content structure according to the current SAP S/4HANA best practices and recommendations. Customers whose SAP Fiori launchpad content is still organized using deprecated tile/target mapping structures must complete a migration project to LADIs before their custom content can be federated through v2. Depending on the volume and complexity, especially on S/4HANA 2023 system, this migration can be substantial. The 2025 release introduces significant enhancements that greatly reduce the required effort; see the documentation for details.

Transaction Code Requirement

Only applications with an assigned transaction code are exposed with v2. While almost all standard apps have been assigned transaction codes as of SAP S/4HANA 2025, some legacy components — e.g. apps shipped with UIHR002 (HCM) or UITRV001 (Travel Management) — cannot technically receive a transaction code. Customers with custom SAPUI5, Web Dynpro ABAP, or WCF apps must create transaction codes for these apps as a prerequisite. For SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01, a downport note for the majority of the missing transaction code assignments to standard apps is under preparation.

User Experience Adjustment

Users accustomed to the in-place model may initially find the new tab-based launching disorienting in the beginning. There is no option for administrators or end users to change this behavior to in-place launching. This is by design but may not suit every organizational preference. SAP recommends enabling the product name display in the shell header so users can immediately identify which system they are working in. Change management and user communication are essential to a smooth transition.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Custom Content

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, only SAP-delivered apps are exposed through v2. Custom apps are not exposed due to the missing transaction code. This can be a significant limitation for customers who have extended their cloud environment, but this topic is planned to be addressed in future SAP S/4HANA public Cloud releases.

Theme Consistency

Because apps launch in the respective product shell, any custom theme used in SAP Build Work Zone should also be transported into all connected SAP systems to ensure a consistent visual experience. Also with the old in-place model (v1), apps relied on the Work Zone theme to be available in their source system, but defining an SAP standard theme as a fallback was often sufficient for a consistent experience. The integration layer model however requires more deliberate theme management across systems.

For further restrictions regarding tile types or considerations, please check the exposure note SAP Note 3345119.

 

Mixed-Mode Considerations

If an organization uses both v1 and v2 within the same Work Zone site — for example, v2 for a current SAP S/4HANA system and v1 for a legacy backend — the result is inconsistent app behavior: some apps open in-place while others launch in a new tab. This should be carefully communicated to users and, where possible, minimized through thoughtful content organization.

 

Who Should Adopt This Paradigm?

The integration layer paradigm powered by exposure v2 is best suited for:

  • Customers on SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud or SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2023 FPS01+ who want to leverage the latest integration architecture.
  • Enterprises with multiple SAP backends where a cross-system entry point with centralized search, tasks, and notifications adds clear value.
  • Customers whose SAP Fiori launchpad content follows the latest LADI-based best practices, or who are willing to invest in migration.

The traditional replacement-shell model with exposure v1 remains appropriate for:

  • Older SAP S/4HANA versions (pre-2023 FPS01), SAP ECC, and SAP Business Suite systems
  • Customers with extensive tile/target mapping content who are not yet ready for LADI migration
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition customers who need custom apps exposed.
  • Existing satisfied customers: If you’re happy with your current setup and your users are accustomed to the existing navigation paradigm, there’s no need to migrate to the new exposure version — the current version will continue to be supported going forward.

 

Conclusion

The positioning of SAP Build Work Zone is evolving from a central launchpad to a cross-product UX integration layer and exposure version 2 is the mechanism that brings this vision to life. By running apps in their native product shells and focusing Work Zone on app access and cross-system services like search, tasks, and notifications, this paradigm delivers a clearer navigation model, full access to product-specific capabilities, and a more consistent app experience.

However, the transition is not without effort. LADI migration, transaction code prerequisites, theme management across systems, and user change management are all factors that require planning. And for organizations with older backends or specific content requirements, the traditional model remains a valid and supported path.

For customers on supported SAP S/4HANA versions, SAP recommends adopting exposure v2 and the integration layer paradigm as the standard approach to cross-product user experience.

 

Further Reading

  • SAP Note 3345119 – Expose Launchpad Content to SAP BTP - Exposure Version 2
  • Content Exposure Versions Overview on help.sap.com
  • Migrating Technical Catalogs to LADIs: Migration Guide and Best Practices
  • SAP Note 3042853 – Limitations for in-place app launching (exposure v1)

entry.title: Increase user productivity with personal My Space in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
Today I’d like to share some really exciting news about new the personalization capabilities in SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.

Motivation
